coredata.dc:description: Photodynamic Therapy (PDT) is an alternative cancer treatment based on the synergistic action of photosensitizer (PS), oxygen, and light exposure in a suitable wavelength range. The increased need for PSs with high selectivity, non-toxicity, and the ability to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) led to the introduction of nanotechnology to PDT research. Carbon-based nanomaterials, including fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide, and carbon-based dots, have been used as photodynamic agents alone or in combination with commonly used PSs due to their superb optoelectronic features, biocompatibility, size, and surface properties. The present review addresses basic principles of PDT, along with recent trends and challenges. Furthermore, carbon-based nanomaterials are discussed with an emphasis on vital features for a potential photosensitizing agent. The main focus of this article is the application of carbon-based nanomaterials in PDT, presenting both in vitro and in vivo studies, as well as highlighting current limitations and future directions.

•Possible cell uptake via energy-dependent endocytic mechanisms
•Possible cell death due to heat formation (NIR absorbance)

head.ce:title: Carbon-based nanomaterials in photodynamic therapy of cancer

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photodynamic Therapy (PDT) is an alternative cancer treatment based on the synergistic action of photosensitizer (PS), oxygen, and light exposure in a suitable wavelength range. The increased need for PSs with high selectivity, non-toxicity, and the ability to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) led to the introduction of nanotechnology to PDT research. Carbon-based nanomaterials, including fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide, and carbon-based dots, have been used as photodynamic agents alone or in combination with commonly used PSs due to their superb optoelectronic features, biocompatibility, size, and surface properties. The present review addresses basic principles of PDT, along with recent trends and challenges. Furthermore, carbon-based nanomaterials are discussed with an emphasis on vital features for a potential photosensitizing agent. The main focus of this article is the application of carbon-based nanomaterials in PDT, presenting both in vitro and in vivo studies, as well as highlighting current limitations and future directions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer constitutes the second-leading cause of death worldwide, following cardiovascular diseases. Cancer is an evolutionary disease that affects a lot of cell types and generates mutations in genes. This makes the disease complex and, in parallel, underlines the need for the development of various therapeutic approaches. Surgery, chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and immunotherapy are the conventional treatment modalities for cancer management. Particularly, surgery relies on the removal of cancerous tissue, but it does not secure total tumor elimination, and cancer recurrence is a possible effect. Chemotherapy incorporates cytotoxic drugs to effectively destruct cancerous tissues; however, it lacks selectivity, and normal cells are also affected. This disadvantage is also evident in the case of radiotherapy, which is based on the killing effect of radiation. Contrary to other strategies, immunotherapy deploys the immune system, and it is a highly targeted therapy that reduces the possibility of cancer recurrence. Nevertheless, this weakens the immune system, and patients are prone to infections [1–4].

Several novel therapeutic methods have been developed to deal with cancer, mitigating the risk of recurrence and the damage to healthy tissues, such as phototherapy. In this type of treatment modalities, photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT) are included (Fig. 1 ). PTT lies in the use of light in the near-infrared (NIR) region to generate heat capable of destructing tumors. On the other side, PDT accomplishes tumor suppression with the aid of light, which produces reactive oxygen species (ROS) and initiates cancer cell death pathways [5–7].

The main focus of this review is PDT, which has advantages in the treatment of early-stage cancer, improvement of patients' quality of life, reduction of systemic toxicity, and provision of great cosmetic outcomes, etc. Light, oxygen, and photosensitizer (PS) act synergistically in cytotoxic action inducement selectively in cancerous tissues, whereas the independent action of these factors is safe for both healthy and cancerous cells. PS is a crucial factor for PDT success and researchers are focused on PSs’ development, capable of generating ROS with increased biocompatibility and selectivity for malignant sites [8–11].

A variety of molecules have been used in PDT as PSs with different structures and properties. Typical cases of PSs are hematoporphyrin derivatives, chlorins, phthalocyanines, and porphyrins. Complex synthesis, reduced aqueous solubility, and lack of cancer selectivity are some common constraints of the above PSs, independently of their ROS generation ability. These limitations can be handled with the aid of nanotechnology, which introduces the use of nanoparticles as independent PSs or as carriers for the existing ones. A vital aspect of nanomaterials is the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect that describes the increased selectivity to tumors as a result of special pathophysiological characteristics in cancer cells [12,13].

Carbon nanostructures, commonly known as carbon allotropes, are a typical example of nanoparticles able to be used in PDT. Carbon is the fourth most abundant element in the universe, and it is a major component in all living organisms. Carbon was first evident as amorphous carbon, diamond, and graphite, while recently several carbon nanostructures have been developed, including fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide, carbon-based dots, etc. Carbon allotropes are the result of differences in carbon arrangement, which leads to materials with different structures and properties [14–16].

The implementation of carbon allotropes in biological applications, including PDT, is an intriguing research aspect due to their superb optical and physicochemical properties, facile modification, and biocompatibility. They can be used in PDT independently as PSs or in combination with other molecules. Synthesis routes and chemical functionalization of carbon allotropes affect, to a great extent, their physicochemical and photophysical properties and, thus, their PDT efficiency. The conjugation of traditional PSs with carbon allotropes aims to boost their biocompatibility, water solubility, and cellular uptake. All these foster ROS production by the PSs, while the existence of surface functional groups in nanostructures facilitates their linkage with targeting agents to reinforce cancer selectivity. An additional point of view contains the application of carbon allotropes in combined cancer modalities, including chemotherapy, or in the field of diagnosis, as it is shown in Fig. 2 [10,15,17].

This review covers the fundamental principles of PDT, emphasizing their major components and current challenges. Additionally, carbon-based nanomaterials, including fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, graphene, oxide, and carbon and graphene dots, are examined regarding their structure and their vital properties for PDT. An important aspect of the study is the review of current scientific work on the implementation of carbon-based nanomaterials in PDT for the treatment of various types of cancer, both in vitro and in vivo.

Compared with the traditional treatment modalities, including chemotherapy, surgery, and radiotherapy, PDT offers successful treatment in first-stage cancer, increased specificity in cancerous cells, low side effects, excellent cosmetic outcomes, feasibility for repeated cure, and prolongation of life in patients with inoperable tumors. An additional asset of PDT is the availability of a combined cancer cure with other modalities. PDT could be a valuable tool in cancer treatment, as many mutations in cancer cells cause resistance to other treatments. However, it must be mentioned that PDT lacks the ability to deal with metastasis and is constrained by skin photosensitization, which forces patients to avoid light exposure until the residual PS excretion from the body [8,9,20–22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photophysical and photochemical processes in PDT
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 depicts the main photophysical and photochemical processes, which occur during PDT treatment, commonly known as Jablonski diagram. The photodynamic action initiates with the absorption of light by a PS at a proper wavelength and its excitation in a singlet state from the ground. The singlet state is considered unstable, and PS will return to the ground state through various possible photophysical pathways. It is possible to generate heat or emit fluorescence through the PS transition to the ground state. Another case refers to PS transition in a triplet state, which has improved stability and a longer lifetime. In this state, two types of photochemical reactions may occur. Type I reactions include the interaction of PS with the cellular substrate, leading to electron transfer and the formation of OH∙, O2 ∙-, H2O2, etc. On the other side, Type II reactions describe the energy transfer from molecular oxygen, 3O2, to singlet oxygen, 1O2. Both photochemical types are key elements for PDT success and result in the production of ROS, which interact with biomolecules, affecting their structure and causing permanent damage. The contribution of each reaction type depends on factors such as the type of PS, oxygen concentration, and cellular environment. Generally, Type II is considered the most prominent mechanism, and most PSs follow this type of reaction. The transition of PS from triplet to ground state is possible through heat or phosphorescence. One more option is called delayed fluorescence, which refers to the photon emission as a result of energy absorption from the environment in order to transfer PS from the triplet to the singlet state and then to the ground state [13,20,21,23,24].

A series of biochemical processes occur with the production of ROS, which initiate three cellular death mechanisms: apoptosis, necrosis, and autophagy (Fig. 5 ). To start with, apoptosis is the planned cellular death mechanism with the following main characteristics: cell shrinkage, chromosomal DNA fragmentation, chromatin condensation, and apoptotic bodies’ formation. Apoptosis is initiated when the outer mitochondrial membrane is permeabilized due to the pore opening of the inner mitochondrial membrane, and effector caspases 3 and 7 are activated. Effector caspases can also be triggered by exogenous factors, including caspase 8, which is detected at the plasma membrane. During PDT, anti-apoptotic proteins including the B-cell lymphoma 2 (BCL-2) family are disordered, while pro-apoptotic proteins such as BCL-2-associated X (BAX) and mitochondrial proteins such as cytochrome c are overexpressed [21,25–27].

In contrast, necrosis is an accidental cell death pathway and constitutes a result of low energy levels. During necrosis, cellular content and pro-inflammatory molecules are released, and this causes an inflammatory reaction. In the case of necrosis, the plasma membrane loses its structural integrity, and depletion of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is observed. A higher dose of PDT in terms of PS's concentration and light fluence is required to achieve cellular death via necrosis. This makes PDT more efficient when apoptosis is activated [21,25–27].

Autophagy balances the synthesis, destruction, and recycling of cell organelles. However, cellular death via autophagy is possible with the destruction of cell organelles initiated by ROS production. Particularly, autophagy includes the formation of an autolysosome, which is a lysosomal vesicle with a lipid bilayer membrane containing damaged cellular components that are further degraded. Autophagy can act as a PDT death mechanism only after a certain level of cellular damage, because the existence of autolysosomes in dying cells may also indicate attempts for cell survival and does not guarantee the prominence of autophagy as a cell death mechanism. For instance, cell lines with silenced autophagy proteins, such as Atg7, exhibit increased apoptosis and lower cell survival after low-dose PDT compared with cell lines positive in Atg7. In another case, the silence of Atg7 resulted in lower PDT efficiency, indicating the cytotoxic role of autophagy. It has also been reported that there is an increase in autophagy markers with PDT, such as the activation of the LC3-I protein to its form, LC3-II. The balance of autophagy in PDT between damaging and cytoprotective roles is influenced by PS, PDT protocol, and tumor type [25,27–29].

Other cellular death mechanisms associated with ROS generation are pyroptosis, ferroptosis, and cuproptosis (Fig. 6 ). Pyroptosis is a lytic and inflammatory regulated cell death pathway that is initiated by inflammasomes and facilitates the triggering of an immune response. This is associated with the release of various proinflammatory factors, such as immunostimulatory cytokines (interleukin-1β and interleukin-18), lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), etc. Pyroptosis is also characterized by cell swelling, rupture of the plasma membrane, and nuclear condensation. Gasdermin (GSDM) family proteins and caspases promote pyroptosis as a cell death mechanism. At first, a protease called caspase-1 accounts for the cleavage of GSDM-D, leading to the formation of GSDM-N fragments, pores to cell membranes, and then to pyroptosis. The release of inflammatory agents and damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) further develops an antitumor response. PDT can induce pyroptosis given the fact that ROS produced during this treatment can activate inflammasomes like NLRP3. Taking into account that ROS have a short lifetime, it is essential to develop PSs targeting organelles such as mitochondria, lysosomes, and endoplasmic reticulum in order to generate ROS in situ in specific organelles and boost pyroptosis mediated by PDT [31–35].

Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent programmed cell death induced by the generation of ROS and lipid peroxides through the PDT process. Some morphological changes found in ferroptosis are decreased size of mitochondria and mitochondria crista, swelling, enhanced membrane potential, membrane rupture, while no chromatin condensation is observed. At the biochemical level, intracellular glutathione (GSH) possesses a protective role against oxidative stress, catalyzing the reduction of phospholipid hydroperoxides, but GSH is reduced in ferroptosis, resulting in an imbalance between oxidative damage and andioxidant defense. Additionally, the enzymatic action of glutathione peroxidase (GPX4) is inhibited by preventing cysteine uptake through the cystine/glutamate antiporter (system Xc −). PS's concentration is a crucial factor for triggering a specific cell death pathway. Apoptosis and necrosis require low and high concentration of the PS, respectively, to be activated, while an intermediate PS's concentration is necessary for ferroptosis inducement. A recent trend in PDT technology involves the synergistic effect of PDT and ferroptosis to deal with hypoxic tumor microenvironment and limited penetration depth of light by coating nanomaterials with PSs and ferroptosis agonists [35–38].

Cuproptosis is a newly reported form of cell death associated with copper accumulation in cells. Specially in cuproptosis, excessive amounts of copper ions bind to lipoyated enzymes of the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle, resulting in the aggregation of these enzymes, the depletion of iron-sulfur cluster proteins, and finally, cell death. Cancer cells tend to adopt the glycolysis pathway for glucose metabolism and are abundant in glutathione, so cells based on mitochondrial respiration are more susceptible to cuproptosis in comparison with the counterparts undergoing glycolysis, and also decreased levels of glucose as well as glutathione facilitate cuproptosis as a cell death mechanism. Some researchers employ the use of cupreous nanomaterials along with glucose oxidase (GOx) that reduces glucose levels in cancer cells by catalyzing glucose oxidation to hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and gluconic acid. PDT can be enhanced in synergy with cuproptosis, as this cell death mechanism promotes ROS accumulation and thus strong oxidative stress to eliminate cancer [39–41].

PDT combats cancer via direct cancer cell death and damage to the vascular system. Both anticancer mechanisms are based on the photophysical and photochemical processes of ROS production. Direct cancer cell death is achieved through the mechanisms of apoptosis, necrosis, and autophagy. The following cancer cell death mechanism depends on factors such as fluence rate, drug-to-light interval, cell type, PS's concentration, etc. One paramount aspect is the intracellular localization of the PS, as it affects the activation of the cellular death pathway. Generally, apoptosis is initiated when PS is located mainly in mitochondria, while localization of the PS to the plasma membrane indicates necrosis as the main mechanism [22,25,45].

Vascular shutdown aims to eliminate endothelial cells and cause damage to factors that promote cell division and create new blood vessels. This leads to an insufficient supply of nutrients to cancer cells, and thus vasoconstriction and thrombus formation delay the growth of cancer cells. Commonly, endothelial cells are more susceptible to PDT compared with fibroblasts or muscle cells. This type of anticancer action is based on the tendency of some PSs, such as Verteporfin, Padoporfin, etc., to localize in the vasculature and cause damage to the tumor vasculature after irradiation. Researchers focus on disrupting tumor vasculature via PDT, and this category of PDT is referred to as vascular-PDT, which is investigated as potential therapy for prostate cancer. Targeting of tumor vasculature is a key factor for vascular-PDT success and can be accomplished through PS's linkage with antibodies or nanoparticles, while common targets include integrin ανβ3, CD13, CD276, fibronectin, etc. [21,22,25,45].

Another anticancer mechanism is the inducement of systemic immunity. In contrast with the tendency of traditional treatment modalities to cause immunosuppression, PDT can cause immunogenic cell death (ICD), which is a kind of immune response based on the connection of DAMPs to immune cells. To explain this more, apoptosis, autophagy, and mainly necrosis promote the release of molecules such as DAMPs, which stimulate the immune system. In normal cells, these molecules are protected, and upon cell death, DAMPs are connected with immune cells such as neutrophils, mast cells, macrophages, and dendritic cells via pattern-recognition receptors (PRRs), leading to an inflammatory response. PS and tumor type are the most determining factors for the level and type of DAMPs. High-mobility group box 1 (HMGB1), calreticulin (CRT), ATP, and heat-shock proteins (HSPs) are some useful types of DAMPs for inducing ICD. HMGB1 is translocated to the extracellular matrix from the nucleus after ICD and acts as an inflammatory cytokine, while CRT is translocated to the cell surface from the lumen of the endoplasmic reticulum and activates immune responses. During ICD, ATP is released due to disruption of membrane structural integrity and interacts with receptors such as P2Y2 or P2X7, leading to the release of the proinflammatory cytokine IL-1β, whereas HSPs are translocated to the cell surface and initiate cell phagocytosis. These molecules intend to attract the immune cells, which will repair homeostasis in the damaged area, and T lymphocytes will be activated. T cells are able to recognize and damage cancer cells, as well as remain in circulation for a long time, which allows for systemic immunity. The PDT effect strongly depends on the PS's location during irradiation, as the produced ROS have a short half-life and will interact with biomolecules similar to them [21,22,25,45]. The main routes of PDT anticancer action are summarized in Fig. 7 .

In PDT, PSs are molecules capable of absorbing light and transferring it to adjacent molecules for generating ROS and thus initiating cell death pathways. Therefore, PSs are a key parameter for successful PDT, and the selection of a suitable PS should be based on various aspects such as chemical, biological, photophysical, clinical characteristics, etc. From a chemical and biological perspective, PS can be a natural or synthetic compound that is chemically stable and does not promote mutations. The last depends on PS's tendency to localize in the cell nucleus. The amphiphilic nature of PS is desired as it enables its penetration through lipidic cellular membranes and its transport to the target through blood circulation. PSs should exhibit maximum absorbance in the “optical window” for PDT in the wavelength range of 600–800 nm, securing sufficient tissue penetration. Vital for PDT is the need for PS to conduct Type I and II photochemical reactions. Among these reported photophysical properties, the fluorescence of PS is also a desirable feature, as it offers the possibility of target-tissue visualization and real-time control of dosimetry. The efficiency of PDT is mainly based on PS selectivity to cancerous tissues and under this scope, some PSs exhibit high affinity for overexpressing factors in cancer cells, such as receptors of low-density lipoprotein (LDL). Regarding the clinical application of the PS, it should be noted that absence of dark toxicity and rapid excretion from the body are necessary elements to reduce the side-effects of skin photosensitization. Also, administration of the proposed PS should be facile, involving systemic, oral, or topical administration, and it is necessary to allow the parallel use of other treatment modalities [8,19,21,25,46,47].

A lot of compounds with a variety of structures and properties have been employed as PDT PSs and are mainly classified into generations (Fig. 8 ). The first one includes hematoporphyrin and its derivatives, such as Photofrin (porfimer sodium), which is also clinically accepted. The main constraints of the first generation are complex structure, long-lasting skin photosensitization, and low absorbance in the red region of the spectrum, which results in limited penetration into tissue and lower PDT efficiency. These facts led to the development of second generation of PSs that include phthalocyanines, porphyrins, or partially hydrogenated porphyrins (chlorins, bacteriochlorins) etc. The main feature of this generation is the cyclic tetrapyrrolic structure of the compounds. They are also less toxic, pure compounds, and exhibit absorbance in the optical window, and secure improved biodistribution. However, second generation PSs face enhanced aggregation, low solubility in aqueous environments, and insufficient cancer cell selectivity. Some of these problems can be resolved with the third generation of PSs, which introduces nanotechnology in the synthesis of PSs. In this generation, nanocarriers are developed that are capable of transporting PSs in target tissue with high selectivity, or nanocarriers can act independently as PSs or synergistically with the PSs for ROS production and cancer treatment [11,13,21,22].

The progress in PS’ synthesis is mainly focused on the enhancement of PDT efficiency and tumor selectivity. The last one can be improved with the conjugation of PSs with targeting agents with high affinity for cancer cells. Some examples of these agents include antibodies, proteins, or carbohydrates. A new approach in PDT is the development of PS, which is activated after a physical and biological stimulus. Physical stimulus describes the PS activation after the application of electric or magnetic fields, ultrasounds, etc., while biological stimulus refers to the recognition of cancer physiological conditions by the PS, including temperature, pH, or enzymatic processes. This strategy aims to improve PDT selectivity for tumor tissues [11,21,46].

Light is a major component of PDT, and thus the success of the therapy is also affected by the PS's activation by light and light-tissue interaction. This interaction is influenced by light features and tissue components such as hemoglobin, elastin, collagen, melanin, etc Depending on the PS, different wavelength is selected for its photoactivation and its selection is vital for light depth penetration into tissue (Fig. 9 ). Particularly, UV light (∼350 nm) hardly penetrates up to 150 μm, while blue light (∼440 nm) is able to penetrate up to 1 mm. Green (∼540 nm) and red light (∼650 nm) reach up to 3 mm and 5 mm, respectively. The therapeutic window has a range of 600–1200 nm and refers to the optimal wavelength range for efficient light transfer in deep-lying target tissues. In lower wavelength values (below 600 nm), it is observed the absorbance from endogenous molecules such as hemoglobin, while wavelength values higher than 800 nm do not secure sufficient energy for PDT action initiation due to strong water absorption, and it is suggested the upconversion of photons to improve the singlet oxygen quantum yields. Based on this concept, most PSs exhibit an absorbance band in the range of 600–800 nm. The use of light in this range does not secure efficient treatment with PDT, as tumor size may surpass the available maximum penetration depth and more treatment sessions may be needed. In addition to this, tumor location may not allow the accessibility of the light source, which justifies the widespread application of PDT in superficial lesions such as squamous and basal cell carcinoma, bladder cancer, ovarian cancer, etc. [8,13,25,49–51].

Lasers, lamps, and laser-emitting diodes (LEDs), and even sunlight are the most commonly used light sources in PDT, as it is shown in Fig. 10 . The selection of the appropriate light source depends on the PS, tumor site, and required light dose. Lamps were the first light source used for PDT, and some used types include fluorescent, incandescent, metal halide, xenon arc, etc. Lamps show a broad emission spectrum (wavelength range: 300–1200 nm), and their use with filters allows the removal of undesirable wavelength values in the ultraviolet and infrared range as well as matching with the absorption band of the PS. In addition to this, lamps’ intensity is considered adequate for the conduction of the required photochemical processes for PDT, and lamps usually have a portable size. Lamps also offer broad illumination and are not expensive. However, lamps have low beam quality, a large beam size, and poor power density, and these features constrict their coupling with optical fibers and limits their use in superficial lesions. The direction of lamps can be tuned with the aid of light guides, but light loss in coupling has been observed. This limits the application of lamps mainly to superficial lesions [21,25,49,50].

Cancer treatments for deeply seated tumors, such as colon cancer, can be achieved with the use of a laser as a light source. In this case, coupling with optical fibers is possible, and the laser has a strong power output with high light intensity at the tip of the fibers, which is capable of inducing photodynamic action. Except for the interstitial application of lasers in PDT, this light source can also be applied to cure superficial lesions with the aid of beam-expanding lenses. The role of lenses is to secure uniform illumination of the target surface. It must be mentioned that lasers are monochromatic, and this makes it necessary to maintain the consistency of laser wavelength emission with the narrow absorption band of PSs. In the case of the use of various PSs with differences in absorption bands, it is important to ensure the existence of appropriate laser devices, which implies an increase in cost of the therapy [21,25,49,50].

Two main laser types have been employed in PDT: diode lasers and dye lasers. Diode lasers are semiconductor devices with wide applications in clinical PDT. They are based on light generation through the recombination of electron-hole pairs. Electrical current is utilized as an energy source. Some advantages of this laser type include stability, small size, and low cost. High-power diode lasers are required for clinical PDT, and this can be accomplished with a combination of laser diodes. However, diode lasers are monochromatic, which insinuates the need for various diode lasers in order to use multiple PSs with differences in absorption bands. Dye lasers employ an organic dye molecule such as rhodamine as a gain medium, which is in constant motion to inhibit overheating. In this type of laser, light is emitted in the range of 600–650 nm, in accordance with PS’ requirements. Pump lasers, including argon or Nd-YAG, are the energy sources used for dye lasers. Also, the dye's absorption wavelength should match the emission wavelength of the pump laser, while it is encouraged to use of optical filters to control the wavelength of the output light. Dye lasers are considered a versatile device as they allow the control of wavelengths in order to be adapted in combination with various PSs. On the other side, this source requires a high-current and voltage power supply and water cooling. In addition to this, dye lasers need high maintenance, are not portable, and are not cost-effective [50].

One simple and efficient option for a light source are the semiconductor devices LEDs, which can have a sufficient fluence rate and are possible to be coupled with optical fibers. Similar to diode lasers, LEDs are based on the recombination between electron and hole to generate light, but they have a lower power output. LEDs possess large beam divergence and broad spectra width, which decreases the efficient light absorption from the PS. In addition to this, there is a concern regarding the thermal effect at deeper sites due to low electrical to optical conversion efficiency. Despite these considerations, it must be mentioned that LEDs are low-cost and portable. LEDs are applied in clinical applications mainly to treat superficial tumors, whereas there is the availability of LED arrays in interstitial PDT [49,50].

The efficiency of PDT is focused on the selection of the appropriate PS and light source; however a light delivery device is also vital for tumor elimination. The choice of light delivery device mainly depends on the target location, because there is the option of surface illumination for superficial lesions, broad beam illumination in the case of intraoperative PDT, or interstitial PDT for deeply seated tumors. Optical fibers are widely used to make light delivery devices flexible, while diffusing tips are applied to secure uniform illumination in the target tissue without approaching normal tissue. The delivery devices contain diffusers of different shapes, such as spherical, balloons, or cylindrical, and have been used to treat cancer at various sites, including the lung, prostate, breast, etc. [49,50].

Light fluence describes the received energy in a certain area of an irradiated spot and is expressed in J/m2, while light fluence rate refers to the light intensity over time per area in W/m2. Lower fluence rates are considered to lead to better antitumor action as a result of increased oxygen availability at the malignancy site. To explain this more, a higher fluence rate induces the rapid conversion of molecular oxygen to singlet oxygen, which causes oxygen depletion in the tumor site and thus a lower PDT effect. At this level of fluence rate, oxygen consumption cannot be compensated rapidly with oxygen by diffusion from the vasculature during PDT. In addition to this, a high fluence rate may promote the overexpression of epidermal growth factor receptors (EGFR), which enhance tumor growth and provide lower efficacy than high fluence rates. These observations vary depending on the type of cancer, PS, light source, distance of the light source, and time. It has also been considered that a low fluence rate promotes the cellular death mechanism of apoptosis, which is preferable to inflammation caused by necrosis [21,25,50,53,54].

Oxygen and the time interval between PS's administration and its photactivation constitute two additional important parameters in PDT. Oxygen secures ROS production via PS transport to a triplet excited state and the execution of energetic processes. Oxygen content varies even between areas of the same tumor, as it is highly dependent on vasculature density. During irradiation, oxygen is depleted, and it is possible to measure it with the aid of oxygen electrodes, sensors with imaging factors, hypoxic cell fraction after the treatment [9,21].

After drug administration, it is vital to retain a time interval before proceeding with the irradiation. This drug-to-light interval depends on PS pharmacokinetics and the necessity of damaging the tumor site or even the vascular system. A short drug-to-light interval indicates mainly vascular damage, while a longer interval allows the diffusion of PS from normal cells and the specificity to cancerous ones, leading to the limitation of long-lasting skin photosensitization [8,25].

PDT dosimetry describes the interaction between light, PS, and oxygen, and it is vital for defining the delivered dose to the tumor. Another important aspect of PDT dosimetry is light-tissue interactions that strongly affect light distribution into the tissue and the amount of light that finally reaches the target tissue. The dose of PDT refers to PS's concentration and light fluence and can be utilized to estimate PDT dosimetry. An indirect method of dose definition describes the measurement of photobleaching products via fluorescence spectroscopy. It is also possible to evaluate vascular damage, necrosis, and blood flow via laser Doppler and diffuse correlation spectroscopy. It must be noted that visual evaluation of the lesion facilitates the PDT dosimetry and thus the PDT effect. For instance, imaging the tumor before treatment allows the formation of an effective therapeutic scheme. During the treatment, it is feasible to assess PS's uptake, oxygen content, and glucose metabolism and after treatment, PDT outcomes can be checked. In deeply seated tumors, imaging can be achieved through computed tomography or magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). Imaging can secure the exact site of cancer tissues and normal ones, while it is necessary to monitor the placement of light delivery devices in inaccessible cavities in the case of interstitial PDT. Optical imaging can also reveal several important factors for PDT and thus provide a new understanding of them because these factors exhibit their effects in localized areas. Generally, oxygen and PS's distribution, singlet oxygen production features, blood flow, immune system reaction, and drug-to-light interval are some factors with a dynamic response that influence the PDT dose and should be tailored to each patient. In some cases, such as prostate cancer, real-time dosimetry is applied in order to optimize and adapt treatment [22,50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Current challenges and trends in PDT
ce:section.ce:para: PDT application in research and clinical fields has given prominence to various facts for investigation regarding PSs, treatment sites, and levels of tumor damage. To start with, it is observed that there is insufficient distribution of PS at the tumor site, leading to lower molecules in the lesion and the failure of tumor destruction. Also, less selectivity implies increased PS concentration in normal tissues, which induces skin photosensitization. This challenge can be resolved with the aid of nanotechnology and PS's encapsulation in nanocarriers, which can release PS in a controlled manner. Some types of nanocarriers can be nanoparticles, such as gold nanoparticles, antibodies involving trastuzumab and panitumumab, peptides including albumin and biotin, or extracellular vesicles. Another solution is the conjugation of PS to binding molecules, such as folic acid, whose receptors are overexpressed in some cancer types. The protection of neighboring tissue remains one of the main PDT challenges, and this underlines the need for investigating the molecular mechanisms regarding drug resistance and tumor targeting [22,25,55].

One more PDT challenge constitutes the light attenuation in tissues, leading to a reduction in penetration depth. Reflectance, refraction, absorption, and scattering are the main causes of light attenuation. Refractive indices and the angle of light between the two media determine the phenomena of reflectance and refraction, and can be mitigated by perpendicular irradiation at the interface of media with different refractive indices. Light absorption is another cause of light attenuation, and it must be taken into account the absorption spectra of existing chromophores in tissues such as melanin or hemoglobin because they convert the absorbed light into heat and influence the penetration of light. The most important parameter in light attenuation and directionality is light scattering, which leads to light dispersion and also decreases light intensity and depth of penetration in tumor tissues. Light attenuation causes insufficient tumor destruction when tumor depth is higher than light penetration depth. So, there is an urgent need to define the optimum wavelength range for irradiation. Irradiation at higher wavelength values could decrease light attenuation, but it deprives the required energy for ROS production. Also, an increase in fluence can resolve this issue; however, it must be mentioned that it may cause thermal damage to tissue due to high light energy [25,56].

The importance of oxygen in PDT has already been mentioned; however, tumors are hypoxic, and this is one more constraint for PDT application. Researchers synthesize PSs with low oxygen requirements in order to act without serious oxygen restrictions. One case is the use of water-splitting materials, which can convert water to hydrogen peroxide and then to oxygen. Some other PSs make use of abundant hydrogen peroxide in cancer cells and secure its conversion into oxygen. Another approach refers to the use of hypoxia-responsive prodrugs such as tirapazamine (TPZ) in combination with PSs. These prodrugs were transformed into their active form, using tumor hypoxia, and it was proven that they enhanced the PDT effect under normoxic and hypoxic conditions. Also, the enzyme hyaluronidase can be employed to reinforce the oxygen diffusion in tumors, thus increasing the oxygen concentration at the tumor site and inhibiting tumor growth. The application of a low fluence rate could inhibit rapid oxygen depletion, as the vascular shutdown at higher fluence rates does not promote oxygen diffusion at the tumor site. One more option is to employ oxygen carriers such as perfluorocarbons or hemoglobin for direct oxygen transfer to the hypoxic site [21,22,25,56,57].

Successful vascular disruption of tumors is necessary in order to prevent cancer relapse. Some PSs focus on the damage to the endothelial layer of the tumor vasculature, but this does not secure the total damage to the vasculature. A solution to this is the linkage of PSs with inhibitors of phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase (PI3K) or of the protein EGFP-EGF1 to enhance selectivity. The PI3K pathway enhances endothelial cell survival and growth after PDT, so the use of an inhibitor of the anti-apoptotic protein Mcl-1 prevents tumor growth [25].

The failure of total tumor elimination is a possible scenario following the PDT modality, implying the existence of cancerous cells even after the therapy and tumor recurrence. The suggested solution for the referred challenges, as well as the combination of PDT with other cancer treatments can reinforce tumor destruction. For instance, it is widely used to combine the synergistic action of PDT with chemotherapy or immunotherapy. Regarding chemotherapy, a lot of chemotherapeutic agents, including doxorubicin, mitomycin C, and lonidamine, have been used in combination with PS in order to improve tumor destruction [25,56].

Years of research gave prominence to some new types of PDT, which aim to resolve the above challenges and improve PDT efficiency in tumor elimination. Two-photon PDT is based on two-photon excitation, where two photons are absorbed at the same time by the PS. Particularly, two-photon excitation (TPE) is a non-linear optical phenomenon, and it must be mentioned that each photon offers one-half of the excitation energy. This allows the use of near-infrared light, which entails deeper tissue penetration. It has been reported that the synthesis of PSs capable of performing this type of PDT [8,50,58].

Another form of PDT is metronomic PDT (m-PDT) and describes the use of low doses of light and PS over a long period of time. In other words, PS can be administered repeatedly, and it is irradiated at low fluence rates over an extended period, which allows the use of m-PDT in deeply seated tumors. This type of PDT is based on the fact that the rate of cancer cell growth does not exceed the rate of cells dying with m-PDT. It must be mentioned that m-PDT enhances the protection of the surrounding tissue and minimizes resistance to the therapy due to the implementation of low, non-toxic doses. Also, m-PDT promotes the cellular death mechanism of apoptosis in cancer cells, and the low light intensity facilitates the decrease in light source size, which offers the possibility for the implantation of a light source inside the body and its regulation with a near-field communication (NFC) system [8,59,60].

The use of sunlight as a light source introduces a new kind of PDT, referred to as daylight PDT. Daylight-PDT is widely applied in the treatment of skin lesions involving actinic keratosis as it alleviates pain due to a lower light fluence rate and demands a lower incubation time (30 min) of PS compared with conventional PDT (1–3 h). Another asset of daylight-PDT is the lower requirements for clinical equipment and visits because patients can undergo daylight-PDT at home. The main concern regarding the use of sunlight as a light source is the consistency of light doses. To be more specific, the light dose may vary in terms of spectrum, fluence rate, or weather conditions. The European Society for PDT in Dermatology provides a protocol for daylight-PDT that takes into account some parameters, including weather and geographic location [21,25,50].

The use of nanotechnology in PDT is a recent and promising approach for enhancing treatment efficiency and constitutes the main issue of this review article. The main aspects of nanomaterials' application in PDT are summarized in Fig. 11 . Nanomaterials are employed to act as nanocarriers of commonly used PSs. In this case, nanospheres or nanocapsules are used to encapsulate or immobilize the PS in order to transport it to the tumor area. This is of the utmost importance, as effective but hydrophobic drugs can be applied in PDT through the use of nanoplatforms and secure improved tumor localization, drug loading, and biocompatibility. Another aspect refers to the use of nanoparticles as independent PS or in conjugation with used PS. The combinational action of PS and nanomaterials aims to enhance ROS production or even provide monitoring through nanomaterials’ fluorescence. Nanomaterials possess some advantageous features for implementation in PDT, including enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect. This effect describes the pathophysiological conditions in tumors that allow the localization of nanomaterials in tumor cells. Additionally, nanomaterials achieve high drug loading due to their large surface-to-volume ratio and improved uptake in cancer cells as a result of facile functionalization with targeting agents. Their small size allows them to bypass immune system obstacles and improve further cell uptake. Some commonly used nanomaterials in PDT are gold nanoparticles, silver nanoparticles, silica nanoparticles, copper sulfides, carbon-based materials, etc. [8,11,22,61].

Carbon can form materials with various properties depending on the arrangement of its atoms and the molecular shape. As a consequence of their non-toxicity, facile surface functionalization, and superb physicochemical properties, carbon allotropes have sparked research interest in their application in biological fields. This section is focused on carbon allotropes’ usage as PSs in PDT, either independently or in combination with commonly used PSs.

Fullerenes are a carbon allotrope with the structure of a truncated icosahedron composed of twenty hexagons and twelve pentagons. Harold Kroto, Richard Smalley, and Robert Curl isolated fullerene in 1985 as a result of the evaporation of graphite atoms with a laser in a helium atmosphere. Mass spectroscopy revealed C60 as the most prominent and stable form of fullerene. This invention was honored with the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1996, and since then, various synthetic methods for fullerenes’ production on a large scale have been developed, such as the pyrolysis of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, graphite electrode sublimation, or charcoal. Except for C60, there have been reports of other forms of fullerenes with different numbers of carbon atoms, including C70, C50, C32, C36, C28, etc (Fig. 12 ) [62–65].

Fullerenes possess some attractive features that facilitate their application in the field of PDT. Particularly, fullerenes are photostable molecules and more resistant to photobleaching compared with commonly used PSs. Additionally, fullerenes follow reactions Type I and Type II, while most PSs, including those with tetrapyrrole structures, follow only Type II. Fullerenes act as catalysts for ROS generation with the aid of illumination. The type of solvent is important for the PDT effect induced by fullerenes. For instance, fullerenes dissolved in organic solvents in the presence of oxygen can form singlet oxygen via energy transfer from the triplet excited state of fullerene. Conversely, ROS can be produced by fullerenes' irradiation in the presence of polar solvents and reducing agents such as NADH. During photochemical processes, fullerenes are considered to be electron acceptors and can receive up to six electrons. This allows their participation in electron transfer reactions. For example, reduced fullerenes can transfer electrons to molecular oxygen to form superoxide radicals. ROS production and quantum yield can be enhanced with fullerenes’ attachment to light-harvesting molecules. In the field of targeted drug delivery, a self-assembled vesicle called the fullerosome is applied, which exhibits increased drug delivery ability and selectivity for cancer tissue [64,65,67,68].

The use of fullerenes in PDT is limited by various factors, such as their hydrophobic nature. This highlights the imperative need for fullerenes’ functionalization in order to improve their water solubility. The spherical structure of C60 facilitates its functionalization with the attachment of hydrophilic chains or the incorporation of atoms, ions, or clusters in its hollow interior. The addition of hydroxyl groups is the most common strategy for constituting fullerenes that are hydrophilic. In general, for this purpose, it is encouraged to implement cationic functional groups, as these groups can be linked with anionic residues in cancer cells, and thus localization to tumor tissue is increased. Some commonly used hydrophilic agents are polyethylene glycol (PEG), silica, carboxylic acids, human albumin serum, dendrimers, and micelles. Another option refers to the attachment of C60 and C70 with sugars, while the conjugation with sugars can also increase tumor selectivity [67–71].

In contrast with conventional PSs, fullerenes exhibit absorbance in the ultraviolet region of the spectrum. However, PDT desires the use of red light to inhibit scattering and absorbance phenomena of the tissue, and thus the in vivo application of fullerenes’ in PDT is constrained. This limitation can be resolved with the use of light-harvesting antennae or with two-photon PDT [67,68,70].

Taking into account the low water solubility of fullerenes and their absorbance in the UV region, non-functionalized fullerenes are not attractive PSs for PDT. Functionalization of fullerenes can be conducted via two main routes: the Bingel-Hirsch reaction and the Prato reaction, as it is evident in Fig. 13 . The first case refers to a two-step cyclopropanation in which methanofullerene is formed. The Prato reaction describes the 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of azomethine ylides to double bonds to generate pyrrolidines [63,64].

An important aspect of PDT is the efficient cellular uptake of PS, because extracellular ROS generation is not sufficient for cancer cell death. Fullerenes do not exhibit fluorescence, and this inhibits the monitoring of cell uptake. Radiolabeled fullerenes or indirect immunofluorescence with antibodies have been used to study the uptake of fullerenes in mitochondria and cell membranes. Energy-filtered transmission electron microscopy and electron tomography have been used for monitoring cellular uptake from the nucleus and lysosomes. Another parameter for the use of fullerenes in PDT involves potential accumulation and slower excretion from the body. Also, it must be mentioned that fullerenes are considered non-toxic agents [67,68].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro application of fullerenes in PDT
ce:section.ce:para: Grebinyk et al. investigated the use of non-functionalized fullerene upon light irradiation with LEDs of various wavelengths (365 nm, 405 nm, 515 nm, and 650 nm) to deal with human leukemic cells (CCRF-CEM). The PS's cell uptake and distribution were evaluated with fluorescent immunostaining via FITC-labeled antibody against C60, nucleus marker DAPI dye, and mitochondrial marker MitoTracker Orange. The use of FITC-labeled antibody revealed that fullerenes can be successfully distributed in the cells after 24 h of incubation because the green fluorescence of FITC was absent in the case of control samples. Fullerenes can penetrate the cell membrane and are detected in the perinuclear region of the cytoplasm, as shown by DNA labeling with DAPI. It was also confirmed that fullerenes are able to localize in the mitochondria of CCRF-CEM cells (Fig. 14 ). Regarding the study of different wavelengths, it must be mentioned that the use of ultraviolet light (365 nm) is not suggested due to increased cell toxicity without the incubation of PS. The in vitro PDT with C60 (20 μM) and irradiation with violet light (405 nm, light fluence: 10 J/cm2) resulted in cancer cell viability of about 50 %, while higher cell viability was recorded upon fullerenes irradiation with green (515 nm) and red (650 nm). This observation is attributed to the decrease in fullerenes' absorbance with the shift in longer wavelength values. It was also confirmed that the examined fullerene induced apoptosis in CCRF-CEM cells through the exposure of phosphatidylserine to the outer plasma membrane and the activation of caspase 3/7 [73].

Most studies regarding the use of fullerenes in PDT are focused on fullerenes' functionalization with various chemical groups in order to improve aqueous solubility and cancer cell selectivity. For instance, functionalized fullerenes have been used as PSs in the following murine cell lines: reticulum sarcoma cells (J774), Lewis lung cancer cells (LLC), and colon adenocarcinoma cells (CT26). Specially, fullerenes with 1, 2, or 3 diserinol groups (hydrophilic groups) and 1, 2, or 3 quartenary pyrrolidinium groups (cationic groups) were synthesized, and their PDT action was investigated at 2 μM concentration upon white light irradiation (400–700 nm, 1–80 J/cm2, 150 mW/cm2). Monopyrrolidinium fullerene was the most efficient PS for all tested cell lines due to its relative hydrophobic nature and its single cationic charge. These features allow improved cellular uptake and mitochondrial localization. Additionally, the functionalization with cationic groups led to more efficient photodynamic performance compared with the fullerenes’ functionalization with hydrophilic ones, while the substitution degree of cationic or hydrophilic groups attached to the PS was inversely proportional to its photodynamic action [74].

In the research of Sperandio et al., the use of functionalized fullerene C70 and fullerene oxide C84O2 to treat human cervical cancer (HeLa cells) with PDT was examined, as well as the role of electron transfer mechanisms in PDT efficiency. In particular, a decationic chain was attached to improve water solubility and affinity with cancer tissues, and a deca-tertiary amine chain was employed to offer electrons for the facilitation of photochemical processes. So, molecules LC-17 and LC-19 were synthesized when C70 and C84O2 were attached, respectively, to a decationic chain, and LC-18 and LC-20 were produced when C70 and C84O2 were attached respectively to both a decationic chain and a deca-tertiary amine chain. The irradiation with UVA light (350–380 nm, 4 mW/cm2, 1,2,4,8 J/cm2) and blue light (390–420 nm, 50 mW/cm2, 10, 20, 40, 80 J/cm2) proved that the addition of the deca-tertiary amine chain enhanced the PDT effect. Fullerenes irradiated with green light (525–555 nm, 80 mW/cm2, 10, 20, 40, 80 J/cm2) resulted in equal response, while irradiation with red light (615–645 nm, 100 mW/cm2, 10, 20, 40, 80 J/cm2) presented LC-17 and LC-19 as the most efficient PSs. This observation implies the fact that shorter irradiation wavelengths promote the conduction of electron transfer procedures. The cell uptake and intracellular localization of LC-19 and LC-20 fullerenes were investigated with the aid of an HPF probe, as fullerenes are not fluorescent materials. The successful uptake of the PSs LC-19 and LC-20 was proved because it was observed increased green fluorescence (Fig. 15 C and E) compared with the control samples (Fig. 15 A). Rhodamine 123 (Rh123) and acridine orange (AO) were the selected probes to study the localization of fullerenes. Rh123 has the tendency to accumulate in the mitochondria of intact cells and diffuse to the nucleus in case of cell death as a result of damaged mitochondrial transmembrane potential. This can be observed in cells treated with LC-19 and LC-20 upon light irradiation (UVA light, 4 J/cm2) in Fig. 15H & I, respectively, compared with untreated cells (Fig. 15 G). Conversely, AO is localized in lysosomes, where it emits green and red fluorescence in living cells (Fig. 15 J and K). In the case of PDT-damaged cells, fluorescence is evidently more intense and dispersed even in the cell nucleus (Fig. 15L–O) [75].

The treatment of HeLa cells with the aid of fullerenes was also examined in the research of Liu et al. In this study, carboxylic acid derivatives of fullerenes called carboxyfullerenes were investigated as potent PSs upon irradiation with white light (400–700 nm) for 30 min and a light fluence rate of 20 mW/cm2. Dimalonic acid, trimalonic acid, and quadrimalonic acid were the carboxylic acids used to form carboxyfullerenes with C60 and C70. The PDT efficiency of the carboxyfullerenes at a concentration of 2.5 μM was dependent on cage size and modification of the cage. Especially, C70 carboxyfullerenes were more effective in killing HeLa cells compared with the correspondent C60 because the extended π-conjugate system of C70 enables them to absorb light in the “optical window” of PDT. Regarding the fullerenes' functionalization, it must be mentioned that fullerenes modified with trimalonic acid resulted in lower cell viability in each case. The PDT performance of trimalonic acid-modified C70 was comparable to that of the traditional non-water-soluble PS, protoporphyrin IX. The study of the photoxicity mechanism of trimalonic acid-modified C70 concluded that this PS followed the Type II photochemical reaction pathway and led to bleb generation due to increased osmotic pressure, while the prominent cell death mechanism was necrosis. The existence of carboxyl groups in fullerenes' structure induces conjugations with targeting agents, which can further enhance affinity for cancer cells [76]. For instance, the trimalonic acid-modified C70 was conjugated with aptamer R13 to treat the human lung cancer cell line A549 upon irradiation with white light (400–700 nm, 20 mW/cm2) for 30 min. The selection of aptamer R13 is based on the expression of EGFR by A549 cells, while its conjugation with fullerenes does not affect the aptamer's ability to bind to cancer cells. The PDT treatment with trimalonic acid-modified C70 or aptamer alone did not cause any major toxicity in cancer cells (Fig. 16 a). On the other hand, the trimalonic acid-modified C70 conjugated with aptamer resulted in a significant decrease in cell viability at a concentration of 5 μM, and its PDT performance was dose-dependent, as an increase in PS's concentration led to a decrease in cell viability (Fig. 16b). The proposed PS is localized in the cytoplasm and in the lysosomes, which enables easier production of intracellular ROS, whereas genomic DNA is protected due to the absence of localization in the nucleus [77].

In some studies, the interest is focused on the conjugation of fullerenes with other commonly used PSs. For example, fullerene C60 can be conjugated via Bingel reaction with the dye BODIPY to act together for the cure of human chronic myelogenous leukemia suspension cells, K562. The role of fullerene is to enhance the intersystem crossing of BODIPY. The PS was further functionalized with glucose via click chemistry reactions to increase hydrophilicity and affinity for cancer tissue. The proposed amphiphilic nanocarrier does not exhibit dark toxicity in K562 cells at a concentration of 10 μM, while it is PDT efficient upon irradiation with a LED source at 630 nm [78]. One more case is the attachment of light-harvesting molecules in a cationic lipid membrane incorporating fullerenes (LMIC60) (Fig. 17 ). This membrane with the attached fullerenes proved to be cytotoxic at lower wavelength values (350–500 nm), but the attachment to the light-harvesting molecules secured the absorbance in the desired wavelength range for PDT of 600–700 nm. This facilitates the transport of capable energy to the fullerenes in order to produce ROS. The increased PDT action was studied in HeLa cells upon irradiation at 610–740 nm with a light fluence rate of 9 mW/cm2, using a xenon lamp as a light source, along with a long-pass filter with a cut-off of 510 or 610 nm. It must be mentioned that the half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of the proposed PS was comparable with that of the commercial PS Photofrin [79].

Except for the light-harvesting molecules, the use of gold nanoparticles (GNPs) in conjugation with fullerenes for PDT has also been reported (Fig. 18 A). In this case, hybrid nano-PS based on GNPs and C60 was synthesized with increased light stability, oxygen independence, and the capability of absorption in the near-infrared (NIR) region. Fullerenes were also functionalized with –NH2 groups to reinforce water solubility. The PDT performance of C60-GNPs was examined in the human breast cancer cell line MCF-7 (Fig. 18 B-Ε), and the use of this PS at a concentration of 10 μg/mL resulted in cell viability of 71.2 % upon irradiation (laser 532 nm, 100 mW/cm2, 10 min). It must be mentioned that GNPs cannot act as PS alone, or that the independent use of C60 resulted in lower cell viability (66.7 %), compared with the correspondent of hybrid. In contrast, the use of laser irradiation at 808 nm (1 W/cm2, 10 min) makes C60-GNPs the most efficient PS, with cell viability of 63.2 %. It should be noted that PDT action was dependent on PS concentration and laser power density [80].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo application of fullerenes in PDT
ce:section.ce:para: Fullerenes have also been studied in vivo as PSs to treat various types of cancer. For instance, the use of functionalized fullerenes in intraperitoneal PDT (IP-PDT) is presented. This type of PDT can be applied to the treatment of peritoneal cancer due to its facile accessibility to cavities with optical fibers and increased tumor selectivity [81,82]. Herein, the functionalized N-methylpyrrolidinium-fullerene (BB4) was formed in micelles using Cremophor EL in order to reduce fullerenes' aggregation and improve their hydrophilicity. In vivo studies were conducted in mice bearing CT26 tumors using a skin flap model instead of light delivered with optical fibers (Fig. 19 ). The mice were treated with BB4 at a concentration of 5 mg/kg and illuminated with white light (400–700 nm), red light (635 nm), and green light (540 nm) with a fluence rate of 100 mW/cm2. In the case of white light irradiation, mice survived for over 7 days compared with the control group, while in vivo PDT with red light led to systemic toxicity due to extended light penetration in abdominal organs. The use of green light allowed mice to survive for over 3 days compared with the control group. Necrosis was considered the most prominent cell death mechanism in vivo as a result of intraperitoneal delivery of the PS instead of intravenously. This also justifies the observation of surviving tumor cells’ aggregation around tumor vessels, which may cause tumor regrowth. To resolve this, it is suggested to apply a PDT scheme with intraperitoneal and intravenous injection at proper drug-to-light intervals [83]. Another case of IP-PDT involves the use of functionalized hexa(sulfo-n-butyl) [60]fullerene, FC4S, to treat sarcoma. Studies were conducted in S180 tumor-bearing mice with laser irradiation at 514.5 nm and light fluence of 100 J/cm2 for 10 min. The proposed PS decreased tumor volume for high-dose PDT (15 mg/kg FC4S), moderate-dose PDT (10 mg/kg FC4S), and low-dose PDT (5 mg/kg FC4S) to 17 %, 17 %, and 20 %, respectively, compared with the tumor volume of the control group. Based on hematology and blood biochemistry studies of mice untreated and treated with PDT, it was observed that mice exhibited an increased level of leukocytes and an improved hematopoietic system with cytokines release after PDT [84].

PEG is widely used as a functionalization agent of fullerenes in order to ameliorate their water-solubility and their selectivity to cancer cells due to the EPR effect. The research of Liu and Tabata evaluates the role of the terminal structure and molecular weight of PEG attached to fullerene in their PDT action. An increase in the molecular weight of PEG led to an improvement in tumor suppression in mice with Meth AR1 tumors growing at light irradiation with a halogen lamp (400–505 nm, 89.2 mW/cm2, 53.5 J/cm2, 10 min). This lies in the fact that an increase in PEG molecular weight led to improved accumulation of PS in tumor tissue. Fullerenes attached to PEG with methoxyl, hydroxyl, and carboxylic groups suppressed mice tumor at a similar level, while the use of an amino-terminal group exhibited the lowest in vivo antitumor activity. The PDT performance of fullerenes functionalized with amino-terminal groups can be explained by their low tumor accumulation in cancer areas and thus their rapid excretion from the body [85]. Fullerenes’ functionalization with PEG was also highlighted by Liu et al. Except for PEG, fullerenes were also functionalized with gadolinium ions (Gd3+) via metal chelation as a magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) contrast agent. This is of the utmost importance, as MRI is a valuable tool for cancer visualization, and current PSs cannot be used as MRI agents. Meth AR1 tumor-bearing mice were employed for the conduction of PDT (halogen lamp, 400–505 nm, 89.2 mW/cm2, 53.5 J/cm2, 10 min), and studies revealed that drug-to-light interval was a key factor for successful tumor suppression, as it is shown in Fig. 20 . Particularly, light irradiation at least 3h after PS injection led to significant PDT action against sarcoma. Additionally, the proposed PS exhibited enhanced MRI signal in tumor visualization compared with the commercial MRI contrast agent Magnevist [86].

Similar research encourages the formation of a nanoplatform capable of combining drug delivery, MRI, optical imaging, PDT, and radiofrequency (RF) thermal therapy (RTT). The last aims to convert radiofrequency to heat capable of destructing tumors. Fullerenes can accomplish this combinational action after proper modification. Shi et al. reported fullerenes functionalized with magnetic nanoparticles such as iron oxide nanoparticles (IONP) to enable the PS potent MRI contrast agent. Fullerene was further functionalized with PEG and folic acid to improve water solubility and cancer selectivity, respectively (Fig. 21 A). Cancer therapy was implemented in mice with S180 tumor growth and the combinational action of PDT (laser: 532 nm, 100 mW/cm2, 10 min) and RTT (13.56 MHz RF, 300 W, 20 min) showed improved antitumor action with the use of C60-IONP-PEG-FA as PS compared with the independent application of these therapeutic modalities. Even more enhanced PDT performance was recorded when a magnet was applied to the tumor site (C60-IONP-PEG-FA/RTT + PDT/magnet) as a result of the nanoplatform's magnetic properties and thus increased tumor selectivity (Fig. 21B). These results were also confirmed with the study of apoptotic cells through the TUNEL (terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase-mediated deoxyuridine triphosphate nick end labeling) assay (Fig. 21D). The proposed nanoplatform was non-toxic, as was evident from the normal increase in mice's body weight during the treatment [87] (Fig. 21C).

In the literature, there are some studies that are focused on the in vivo PDT application of fullerenes in conjugation with conventional PSs. The research of Li et al. proposed the conjugation of fullerenes with the PS 5-aminolevulinic acid, 5-ALA. The action of 5-ALA lies in its metabolism into PpIX, which constitutes a vital photodynamic agent. However, the application of 5-ALA in PDT is constrained by its hydrophobic nature, which emphasizes the need to functionalize it with other molecules such as fullerene. In the case of C60-5-ALA, improved tumor elimination was recorded on B16–F10 tumor-bearing mice in comparison with 5-ALA at a concentration of 30 mg/kg upon laser irradiation at 630 nm with 100 mW/cm2 for 0.5 min (Fig. 22 ). No weight loss in mice was reported during the therapy, indicating the safety of the examined PS [88] (see Fig. 22).

Taking into account the fact that glucose penetrates cancer cells more efficiently than normal ones via endocytosis, Otake et al. investigated the PDT efficiency of fullerenes conjugated with glucose. The aim of the glycoconjugation was to fortify fullerenes’ aqueous solubility and cancer selectivity as cancer cells overexpress the membrane protein glucose transporters GLUT. Superficial lesions, such as skin cancer, are the suggested field of application of the proposed PSs, as they are excited by UVA light, which limits the deeper penetration of light. Based on this concept, fullerenes conjugated with glucose were tested in vivo for COLO679 melanoma-bearing mice upon UVA laser irradiation (128 mW/cm2, 10 J/cm2), leading to successful tumor inhibition. However, repeated photodynamic treatment with the fullerenes is suggested, as a rapid increase in tumor growth rate was observed after PDT [89].

In another case, researchers studied the PDT action of fullerenes linked with black phosphorus, BP nanosheets. In general, BP nanosheets present limited use in PDT, due to their low efficacy and deficient stability in the cellular environment. Ball milling was employed for the mixing of fullerenes, C60, and BP, and the shielding effect of hydrophobic fullerenes led to increased stability of the synthesized PSs in fetal bovine serum (FBS), phosphate buffer saline (PBS) and water. Increased ROS production and augmented stability of BP-C60 contributed to higher tumor suppression in 4T1 tumor-bearing mice with a tumor inhibition rate of 88.2 % with laser irradiation at 650 nm (0.5 W/cm2, 10 min) in comparison with the independent action of C60 (30.7 %), BP nanosheets (36.6 %), or the C60/BP nanosheets mixture without ball milling (55.2 %). The proposed PS is non-toxic based on the assessment of the body weight of mice during treatment. Also, BP-C60 caused evident cancer cell nucleus destruction, the highest inhibition in tumor growth, and the appearance of apoptotic cells based on hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining, Ki-67 staining, and the TUNEL assay. Histopathological examination proved the lack of toxicity in the major organs of mice, highlighting the absence of systemic toxicity of the PS [90].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of carbon nanotubes in PDT

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) constitute an allotropic form of carbon and are mainly classified into single-wall carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) and multi-wall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) (Fig. 23 ). CNTs' structure is hollow cylindrical, and it is defined by single or multiple graphene layers, while its arrangement consists of hexagons and pentagons with carbon atoms' diameters of 3–15 nm. SWCNTs consist of a single layer of graphene with a diameter range of 1–2 nm, whereas MWCNTs possess multiple layers of graphene with a diameter of 2–50 nm. The use of catalysts in CNTs' synthesis is considered a vital parameter, and CNTs’ length varies according to the synthesis method. CNTs were found by Iijima in 1991, who synthesized MWCNTs during the arc evaporation method of graphite electrodes. The synthesis of SWCNTs was followed in 1993 with the same technique, adding iron catalyst [12,91–93].

CNTs own a variety of properties, making them attractive candidates for photosensitizing agents in PDT. In particular, CNTs are chemically inert and biocompatible, which are desirable features for their usage in biology-related applications. Additionally, CNTs stand out for their physicochemical properties, their ease of functionalization, which allows modification of their properties, and their facile conjugation with other PSs. It must also be mentioned that CNTs can be linked with targeting molecules such as folic acid, magnetic nanoparticles, antibodies, or fluorescent agents for amplification and monitoring of their action [12,65,94,95].

The physicochemical characteristics of CNTs determine their pharmacokinetics and metabolism. For example, their application in the development of drug delivery systems, tissue engineering, bio-imaging, and diagnosis is mentioned. Regarding the CNTs’ implementation for diagnostic purposes, CNTs are used to diagnose cancer as they are able to generate high signal-to-noise ratios in NIR fluorescence imaging. As nanoparticles, CNTs show the EPR effect, which makes them highly selective for cancer cells. Thus, research interest is focused on the in vitro and in vivo application of CNTs, as well as their usage as drug carriers [65,94–96].

CNTs possess a high surface area that enables easier drug loading in their inner structure to secure delivery to target tissue and successful cancer cell uptake. The needle-like shape of CNTs allows intracellular penetration and drug loading to their surface, regardless of their functionalization and cell type. This is of the utmost importance in drugs that target the blood-brain barrier (BBB). Except for the common CNTs’ penetration through the cellular membrane, cell uptake via energy-dependent endocytic mechanisms has also been reported [94–96].

Chemical functionalization of CNTs is possible with the covalent attachment of hydrophilic functional groups or with the non-covalent addition of amphiphilic macromolecules such as lipids, polymers, and surfactant compounds. Functionalization of CNTs is necessary to increase solubility, reduce toxicity, and provide more attachment sites to chemotherapeutic drugs, PSs, antimicrobial drugs, etc. For example, PEG is used as a functionalization agent to prolong the blood circulation of CNTs and improve bioavailability [65,94–96].

The wide and direct use of CNTs, especially in clinical applications, is limited by their semiconducting nature. CNTs absorb in the NIR region of the spectrum, which encourages their implementation in phototherapy (PTT). However, this implies the danger of cell death due to the formation of heat. There is a variety in size and length of CNTs, and this necessitates their functionalization to achieve special features [65,94–96].

In the literature, there are controversial studies regarding the toxicity of CNTs, and thus there are limited in vivo studies of their use in PDT. It is known that CNTs with unmodified hydrophobic surfaces exhibit enhanced cytotoxicity due to the existence of heavy metals, but this can be resolved with their suitable purification. Also, low water solubility and aggregation are undesirable features that can be dealt with through functionalization. Additionally, it has been recorded that CNTs have toxic effects on their distribution in the peripheral nervous system and blood circulation, as well as inflammation in the lungs after CNTs' exposure. The biological effect on CNTs strongly depends on factors including surface, length, diameter, shape, and aggregation. For instance, it is considered that CNTs with a higher length and a smaller diameter exhibit intense cytotoxicity. CNTs’ toxicity is also related to concentration, and further research is needed to define a safe dose of CNTs [65,94–96].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro application of carbon nanotubes in PDT
ce:section.ce:para: CNTs have been examined as photosensitizing agents in PDT, mainly in conjugation with other commonly used PSs. Some studies underline the improved results of PS chlorin e6 (Ce6) in cancer treatment upon interaction with CNTs. For instance, Xiao et al. proposed the synthesis of SWCNTs-Ce6 complexes with enhanced drug loading capacity as a result of the high surface area of SWCNTs. The examined PS is based on the non-covalent π-π interactions of SWCNTs and Ce6, and chitosan was further employed for the reinforcement of the aqueous solubility and biocompatibility of the complexes. It must be mentioned that a critical step in the preparation of the complexes is the purification of SWCNTs from impurities including catalysts (Fe, Co, Ni), amorphous carbon, fullerenes, etc. before the interaction with Ce6. The in vitro studies were conducted in HeLa cells, which can successfully uptake the complex and free Ce6. The main cellular uptake mechanism of the complex is endocytosis, while HeLa cells take up free Ce6 via diffusion. The complex SWCNTs-Ce6 was more effective than Ce6 alone in HeLa cells treatment upon laser irradiation at 630 nm (150 mW/cm2, 600 s), as complexes showed lower values of IC50 and could be taken up by cells efficiently after 3h incubation [97]. Another recent study presented the formation of complexes based on the non-covalent interactions of SWCNTs and Ce6. In this case, hyaluronic acid (HA) acts as a hydrophilic and stabilizing agent. Fig. 24 depicts the morphological changes in colon cancer cells, Caco-2, with incubation of SWCNTs, Ce6, and SWCNTs-HA-Ce6, before and after PDT treatment. Morphological changes in cells were not observed in the non-irradiated group, but in vitro PDT (laser irradiation: 660 nm, 5 J/cm2 and 10 J/cm2) resulted in cell death. Also, the role of light fluence in PDT performance was examined, concluding that the increase in light fluence led to intense cell death, as it is shown in Fig. 25 . Regardless of the selected light fluence, the PS SWCNTs-HA-Ce6 inhibited proliferation of Caco-2 cells and caused lower cell viability compared with pristine Ce6 as a result of improved cellular uptake and biocompatibility of Ce6 in the complex [98].

Similar studies presented the combinational action of CNTs with traditional PSs, including phthalocyanine derivatives, or BODIPY. One case is the covalent conjugation of SWCNTs with the phthalocyanine derivative zinc mono carboxy phenoxy phthalocyanine, ZnMCPPc, along with the targeting agent spermine, which reinforces the binding of PS to cancer cells. Extended photophysical studies showed that phthalocyanines’ fluorescence is independent of the addition of spermine and SWCNTs. It must be mentioned that spermine strengthens triplet and singlet oxygen quantum yields compared with phthalocyanine alone due to the phenyl bridge between phthalocyanine and spermine, which promotes intersystem crossing. The strong affinity of spermine with DNA intensifies the dark toxicity of spermine-ZnMCPPC, while the adsorption of SWCNTs to ZnMCPPC reduces the dark toxicity of the complex as the direct contact between cells and spermine-ZnMCPPC is inhibited. The in vitro studies were conducted in MCF-7 cells with filtered light from an electric quartz lamp (600–1000 nm, 93 mW/cm2, 300–1200 s), and the complex spermine-ZnMCPPC exhibited the highest cytotoxicity, and the lowest one was recorded in the use of ZnMCPPC alone as PS. Treated cells with spermine-ZnMCPPC-SWCNTs showed changes in morphology after PDT, and cell proliferation was impeded [99]. The dye BODIPY is one more PS used in PDT synergistically with CNTs. Particularly, SWCNTs non-covalently interacted with BODIPY via π-π interactions of modified pyrene substituents of BODIPY with the carbon allotrope. The PS BODIPY-SWCNTs showed less generation of singlet oxygen compared with the free BODIPY upon irradiation at 660 nm with a LED array with the use of the molecule 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), which traps the produced 1Ο2. This fact does not inhibit its application in PDT, as the non-covalent pyrenyl-SWCNTs interactions allow the conjugation of targeting molecules on the carrier SWCNTs, resulting in improved cancer selectivity and PDT efficiency [100].

CNTs can also be used in PDT in combination with porphyrins, taking advantage of the CNTs' ability to transfer energy to porphyrins to produce singlet oxygen. The photoinduced charge transfer (PCT), Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) phenomenon, or generally a photon absorption-emission mechanism, transports the recombination energy of excitons from SWCNTs to porphyrin. An exciton (electron-hole pair) is formed as a result of received energy, which is higher than the energy gap of SWCNTs. Porphyrin can transfer this energy to molecular oxygen in order to form singlet oxygen. The proposed hybrid system SWCNTs-porphyrin exhibits higher production of singlet oxygen compared with SWCNTs and porphyrin alone [101]. Similar research highlights the increased ability of SWCNTs-porphyrin in the production of hydroxyl radicals. This type of radicals is produced from singlet oxygen in situ as a result of charge transfer from porphyrin to singlet oxygen. The improved radicals’ production in the hybrid system is due to the porphyrin action as a conductor between SWCNTs and water. This allows electron penetration into dissolved oxygen in an aqueous medium. The evaluation of various porphyrin molecules conjugated with SWCNTs showed that the charge distance of the peripheral groups with the macrocyclic ring affects the ability of singlet oxygen production.
