In case of molecular charge closer to the hydrophobic porphyrin macrocycle, there is a smaller overlap between the exciton wavefunctions of porphyrins and SWCNTs, resulting in lower performance [102].

Furthermore, CNTs can be applied for cancer treatment via PDT in combination with PTT. For instance, MWCNTs non-covalently interacted with the dye phycocyanin, PC, and water-soluble chitosan, CS. The role of chitosan is to increase the hydrophilicity of the nanotubes as well as facilitate their attachment to the PC. Free PC is photothermally inactive, and the addition of MWCNTs facilitates the conversion of received photons into thermal energy. In vitro studies were carried out in MCF-7 cells and the human liver cancer cell line, HepG2 cells, for PTT (laser irradiation at 808 nm, 500 mW/cm2, 5 min) or PDT (laser irradiation at 532 nm, 500 mW/cm2, 5 min) with the use of MWCNTs-CS, MWCNTs-CS-PC, and PC as PSs. In each cell type, only MWCNTs-CS and MWCNTs-CS-PC induced cell toxicity with irradiation at 808 nm, while MWCNTs-CS-PC and PC were effective in cancer cell killing with irradiation at 532 nm. Especially, the complex MWCNTs-CS-PC showed MCF-7 (HepG2) viability at 18.5 % (31.7 %) and 75.2 % (88.2 %) for irradiation at 808 nm and 532 nm, respectively. The morphological changes in MCF-7 cells incubated with MWCNTS-CS-PC were also examined with or without irradiation at 808 nm. Untreated cells had normal morphology without cell breakage, while cells incubated with MWCNTs-CS-PC became brown, and black materials entered the cellular area. This implies the successful uptake of MWCNTs-CS-PC by cells after 12 h of incubation. The implementation of irradiation at 808 nm for 5 min in cells incubated with MWCNTs-CS-PC led to chromatin condensation and DNA fragmentation, and the cells possessed an irregular shape. Also, PS is decomposed and nanotubes aggregated, and this resulted in the formation of water-soluble black structures, which were released from the dead cells [103]. In another case, Marangon et al. described the use of complexes of MWCNTs with the clinically approved PS m-tetrahydroxyphenylchlorin (mTHPC) to deal with human ovarian cancer cells, SKOV3. The mTHPC was loaded on MWCNTs via π-π stacking, and the cellular uptake of the proposed nanosystem enables release of mTHPC feasible in order to carry out its photodynamic performance. The main advantages of the nanosystem MWCNTs/mTHPC are the enhanced photostability outside the cells, improved cellular uptake, and avoidance of mTHPC release in normal tissues. Also, the thermal activation of the PS in the NIR region is possible, promoting the use of PTT. The conduction of in vitro PDT (laser: 650 nm, 125 mW/cm2, 100, 300 και 500 s) and in vitro PTT (laser: 808 nm, 2.3 W/cm2, 100 και 200 s) showed that MWCNTs/mTHPC affect the metabolic activity of SKOV3 cells. In the case of synergistic PDT/PTT, it is observed that there is a greater effect on the reduction of cancer cell viability at the same concentration of PS (Fig. 26 ) [104].

CNTs have also been implemented in the field of the synergistic action of PDT and chemotherapy. Particularly, CNTs were loaded with the chemotherapeutic drug doxorubicin (DOX) and the targeting agent folic acid. The aim of this nanoplatform was the selective release of the drug, which can be secured by the use of folic acid and reduced pH in cancer cells. In vitro PDT (LED irradiation: 460–485 nm, 115 mW/cm2) was conducted in cell lines that do not express folate receptors, such as the normal cell line (L929) and cancer cell line (A549), and in HeLa cells, which overexpress these receptors. Studies with the fluorescence microscope revealed that L929 and A549 cells incubated with CNT-FA-DOX had lower fluorescence compared with the correspondent of incubated HeLa cells. This implies the more effective cellular uptake of the PS by the folate receptors on the surfaces of HeLa cells via endocytosis, and this led to lower cell viability upon irradiation. It must also be mentioned that the released DOX from the nanoplatform is comparable to that of DOX applied in chemotherapy [105].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo application of carbon nanotubes in PDT
ce:section.ce:para: In literature, there are limited studies for the in vivo use of CNTs as photosensitizing agents. Wang et al. studied the in vivo use of CNTs in B16–F10 type of cancer, emphasizing on CNTs' modification method. In particular, they modified nanotubes with polyethylenimine, PEI (SWCNTs-PEI), and Polyvinylpyrrolidone K 30, PVPk30 (SWCNTs-PVPk30), covalently and non-covalently, respectively. SWCNTs-PEI was more efficient in tumor suppression by 45 % in B16F10 tumor-bearing mice, with a relative tumor volume of 6.48 compared to 7.77 and 11.38 for SWCNTs-PVPk30 and the control sample, respectively. Histological examination proved the existence of necrotic and apoptotic cells with the use of SWCNTs-PEI and SWCNTs-PVPk30 after 10 days of treatment, while no damage was observed without exposure to light [106]. CNTs have been used in vivo as PSs in combination with Ce6 and synergistic PDT/PTT treatment of oral squamous cell carcinoma (SCC-7 cells). In this research, SWCNTs were modified with dispersion agent evans blue and targeting agent albumin in order to increase water solubility and prolong nano-PS's circulation, respectively (Fig. 27 (A) ). The PS SWCNTs/albumin/Ce6 was examined in SCC-7 tumor-bearing mice, and the combined PDT (laser irradiation: 630 nm, 0.15 W/cm2, 10 min)/PTT (laser: 808 nm, 1 W/cm2, 15 min) action resulted in successful tumor destruction without recurrence. Conversely, tumors reemerged 6 days after PTT treatment with PS SWCNTs/albumin. In each case, complexes of SWCNTs and Ce6 with or without albumin exhibited improved cytotoxic action compared with Ce6 alone. Histological examination was also conducted, and no damages were observed in major mice's organs, as it is evident in Fig. 27 (B) [107].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of graphene oxide in PDT

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene Oxide (GO) is a 2-D material, and it is considered a graphene derivative with the attachment of various oxygen functional groups (Fig. 28 ). It was first synthesized by Brodie in 1859, much earlier than the discovery of graphene. Single-layer GO was synthesized via the oxidation and exfoliation of graphite. The properties of GO strongly differ from those of graphene, as the existence of oxygen atoms in GO converts sp2 hybridization to sp3. For instance, GO is an electrical insulator, and it possesses groups of oxygen (carboxylic –COOH, hydroxyl –OH, epoxy > O, carbonyl = O, alkoxy C–O–C, etc.), which offer improved water solubility compared with graphene. The reduced graphene oxide (rGO) is a result of thermal and chemical modification of GO for oxygen content reduction. This implies the removal of functional groups from GO and results in a structure similar to graphene with defects and various sizes. An important parameter in the reduction of GO to rGO is the C/O ratio. Also, rGO is considered an electrical conductor and shows low dispersion in solvents, forming aggregates [17,63,108–110].

GO can be applied in biological applications, including drug delivery or PDT, due to its high surface area and π-π graphene structure. This is of the utmost importance, as it allows its conjugation with aromatic structures such as PSs. Another asset of GO's use in the biological field is the ease of chemical functionalization that facilitates its attachment with traditional PSs as well as with targeting molecules including peptides, folic acid, hyaluronic acid, etc. In contrast with graphene, GO exhibits hydrophilicity, resulting in enhanced biocompatibility and the avoidance of aggregation formation. Generally, both GO and rGO have been implemented in PDT for the development of nanoplatforms capable of delivering PSs to target tissues [17,63,113].

GO is considered a safe nanomaterial based on in vitro and in vivo studies. The use of GO promotes the synthesis of inert modified nanoparticles with prolonged blood circulation and intense cancer selectivity, aiming to delay excretion from the body. In the literature, it is referred the development of nanosystems based on GO, which induce the synergistic action of chemotherapy with PDT/PTT, taking into account the high drug loading capacity of GO. Regarding this aspect, GO can act as a drug carrier with great stability and as a cellular imaging agent. Some critical factors for the biological action of GO are surface, zeta potential, size, and drug loading efficiency [114].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro application of graphene oxide in PDT
ce:section.ce:para: Both GO and rGO have been applied in PDT in vitro to treat various types of cancer in combination with other compounds. For example, Khan et al. proposed the synthesis of hybrid nanoparticles based on rGO and manganese oxide (Mn3O4) for in vitro PDT in the A549 cell line. The hybrid nanosystem can be potentially used in cancer diagnosis, as Mn3O4 is a commonly known MRI agent. It must be noted that both Mn3O4 and rGO/Mn3O4 did not present dark toxicity, while in vitro PDT with rGO/Mn3O4 at a concentration of 50 μg/mL resulted in 20 % cell viability with laser irradiation at 670 nm with a fluence rate of 0.1 W/cm2 for 5 min. For various concentrations of the hybrid PS, cell viability was lower compared with that of cells incubated with Mn3O4 [115]. Another case involves the conjugation of rGO with titanium dioxide nanoparticles (TiO2 NPs) in order to reinforce TiO2 light absorbance in the UVA and visible region. The PS rGO-TiO2 exhibited less dark toxicity compared with free TiO2 NPs when incubated in HepG2 cells. The application of in vitro PDT to cells incubated with free TiO2 and coupled with rGO showed that rGO enhanced cytotoxic action upon irradiation with visible light (420 nm, 3.7 mW/cm2) compared with UVA light irradiation (365 nm). In addition, the morphology of HepG2 cells was examined under various circumstances (Fig. 29 ). Cells’ exposure to UVA and visible light did not significantly affect cell morphology (Fig. 29b and c). Conversely, cells incubated with free TiO2 NPs and irradiated with UVA and visible light led to changes in terms of adherence and cell density growth for the UVA-irradiated group (Fig. 29d and f). In vitro PDT with rGO-TiO2 resulted in cell nucleus destruction and round and floated cells under UVA light exposure (Fig. 29e), while cell shrinkage and changes in the cellular membrane were recorded under visible light irradiation (Fig. 29g) [116].

Intracellular localization of PS in cell organelles is a major issue for successful tumor destruction with PDT. Some drugs have the tendency to accumulate in lysosomes after their penetration into cells; however, their localization in mitochondria is considered more effective in apoptosis and thus in the PDT activity of the PS. This lies in the fact that damaged mitochondria cannot produce a sufficient amount of ATP, which promotes ROS generation. So, research interest is focused on the development of PSs localized in mitochondria. Wei et al. presented a drug delivery system for the second-generation PS Pyropheophorbide-a (PPa) based on GO. PPa is hydrophobic and lacks cancer cell selectivity, increasing the necessity of functionalization with factors such as PEG, GO, and the monoclonal antibody integrin αvβ3. The localization of the proposed nanoplatform in mitochondria reached 80 % after 8 h of incubation, while PPa was detected in other organelles, including lysosomes. In vitro studies were conducted in cell lines overexpressing this type of antibody, including human glioblastoma U87-MG, and in cell line with low levels of integrin receptors, such as MCF-7 cells, with laser irradiation at 630 nm with a light fluence of 20–30 J/cm2 for 10 min. In the case of U87-MG cells (high levels of integrin receptors), in vitro PDT with GO/PPa/αvβ3 resulted in 70 % cell death, which is higher compared with the correspondent of PPa alone (50 %) due to enhanced drug loading and cancer cell targeting. On the other side, nanoplatform exhibited less PDT action in comparison with PPa in MCF-7 cells (low levels of integrin receptors). This performance can be explained by the fact that the large molecular size of the nanoplatform decreased the penetration of the PS into the cellular membrane [117]. In another study, the synthesis of a porphyrin derivative, meso-tetra(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin, substituted with the mitochondrial targeting agent tri-triphenylphosphonium was reported. This PS (TCPP-TPP) was further attached with folic acid and GO to reinforce selectivity and successful delivery to target tissue, respectively. The proposed nanosystem GF@TCPP-TPP exhibited a stronger PDT effect in HeLa cells with irradiation at 650 nm and a light fluence rate of 5 mW/cm2 for 10 min compared with pristine TCPP-TPP as a result of the GO and folic acid action on the inhibition of aggregation for TCPP-TPP [118].

Some research studies underscore the use of GO in synergistic PDT, PTT, and chemotherapy to treat cancer in vitro. In particular, rGO combined with silver (Ag) was applied in the PDT/PTT treatment of MCF-7 cells. Sodium hydroxide solution was employed to link rGO with Ag in order to accelerate the reduction of GO and Ag was facilitated to restore the sp2 structure of rGO. MCF-7 cells were incubated 24 h with the PS and irradiated with laser at 532 nm, 100 mW/cm2 for 5 min (PDT), and with laser at 808 nm, 1 W/cm2 for 3 min (PTT), resulting in a significant reduction in cell viability [119]. In the study of Chang et al., a hydrogel system containing rGO, spinach extract, and gold nanocages (AuNCs) was proposed for combined PDT, PTT, and chemotherapy (Fig. 30 ). Spinach acts as PS upon light irradiation at 660 nm, increasing biocompatibility and facilitating hydrogel formation. AuNCs exhibited improved singlet oxygen production and have photothermal properties, while their large surface area, biocompatibility, and facile functionalization foster the use of rGO in the proposed hydrogel. In the case of chemotherapy, rGO acts as a drug carrier for the chemotherapeutic drug fluorouracil (5-FU). The formation of hydrogel outbalances other therapeutic forms, as it offers controlled drug release and accumulation in the targeted area. In vitro studies were conducted in HeLa cells upon NIR irradiation (808 nm, 200 mW/cm2), and spinach, rGO/spinach, and rGO/spinach/AuNCs were examined as PSs, resulting in cell viability of 80.2 %, 70.1 %, and 55.4 %, respectively. These studies highlighted the beneficial role of AuNCs and rGO in PDT action, while the nanoplatform rGO/spinach/AuNCs was investigated as carrier of 5-FU. In this case, cell viability of 27.5 % and 1.2 % were recorded with incubation for 24 h and 48 h, respectively, and laser irradiation at 660 nm and 200 mW/cm2 for 10 min [120].

A lot of studies underline the improved results of known PSs when they interact with GO. For example, GO with a negative surface charge has been interacted electrostatically with the cationic PS TMPyP (5,10,15,20-tetrakis(1-methyl4-pyridinio) porphyrin tetra(p-toluensulfonate)). TMPyP is not widely applied in PDT due to its inability to absorb red light, and thus conjugation with GO shifts the absorbance in a desirable wavelength range for PDT, as a result of molecular flattening of TMPyP. The interaction between GO and TMPyP is based on non-radiative charge transfer and leads to the enlargement of π conjugation in the porphyrin system. In vitro PDT was carried out in HeLa cells with LED irradiation at 740 nm with a light fluence rate of 33 mW/cm2 for 15 min and at 414 nm with a light fluence rate of 10 mW/cm2 for 100 s. A significant reduction in cell viability was observed at a concentration range of 3–25 μM of GO/TMPyP, compared with the independent PDT activity of TMPyP. These results were further confirmed with the aid of a confocal fluorescence microscope 24 h after PDT (Fig. 31 ). In the case of incubated cells with GO/TMPyP, the shrinkage of cells with necrotic and apoptotic features is evident, while cells treated with TMPyP exhibited adhesion to the substrate surface [121].

GO has also been recently studied with various derivatives of the PS BODIPY for PDT in the K562 cell line. BODIPY was non-covalently interacted with GO, and BODIPY was initially functionalized with PEG and glucose units to improve water solubility and cancer selectivity, respectively. Specially, heavy-atom-free, brominated, and iodinated distyryl-BODIPY derivatives were investigated as PSs in vitro, as well as their correspondent complexes with GO. All the examined PSs were biocompatible due to the lack of dark toxicity in K562 cells, while in vitro PDT was conducted with LED irradiation at 660 nm with a light fluence rate of 25 mW/cm2 for 8 h. The heavy-atom-free BODIPY derivative and its counterpart with GO did not generate ROS, and thus they did not show significant cytotoxic results in vitro. The addition of GO to BODIPY derivatives resulted in enhanced efficacy in PDT, and it should be noted that GO addition to brominated BODIPY showed even more cytotoxic performance in comparison to the complex with iodinated BODIPY [122]. Another research presented the covalent interaction of GO with BODIPY and PDT reinforcement with the decrease in glutathione levels in cancer cells. The PS is highly selective for glutathione, and glutathione depletion via nucleophilic substitution of GO and BODIPY reduces the intrinsic defense of cancer cells to ROS; therefore, PDT performance is enhanced. So, BODIPY was functionalized properly to obtain the necessary functional groups for glutathione reduction, and in parallel, interaction with GO amplified the biological efficacy. Cytotoxicity was evaluated in 2D and 3D cell models of prostate adenocarcinoma cells (PC-3), which are desirable modes for preclinical studies due to increased cellular organization, the ability to estimate biochemical parameters, and critical architectural factors regarding nanomaterials’ penetration. In vitro PDT (halogen white light source: 732 nm, 5.4 mW/cm2, 150 × 103 J/cm2, 48 min) showed the improved cytotoxicity of GO-BODIPY compared with GO and the lower efficacy of GO-BODIPY in the 3D models compared with the 2D ones. The last observation is justified by taking into account that graphene may have exhibited limited diffusion in the 3D model, resulting in less cellular uptake and interaction between cell and graphene [123].

A lot of references investigate the conjugation of GO with PS methylene blue (MB) for in vitro PDT. MB is water-soluble and can effectively generate singlet oxygen and absorb light in the visible region. In this case, GO acts as a carrier, transferring the PS to the target tissue in a more efficient way and reinforcing the aqueous solubility of MB. The existence of carboxylic groups makes the GO surface negatively charged, and hence the electrostatic interaction with cationic molecules, including MB, is encouraged [124–126]. The complex GO-MB exhibited improved singlet oxygen production compared with free MB upon laser irradiation at 785 nm for 55 min. Also, the addition of GO reduces complex fluorescence, offering supplementary energy to the MB transition of the singlet to triplet state (intersystem crossing). This fosters singlet oxygen production from the complex [124]. Complexes of GO with MB have been tested as PS in vitro in the human breast cancer cell line MDA-MB-231 with LED irradiation at 630 nm with a light fluence rate of 30 mW/cm2 for 30 min. The complex was more PDT efficient with cell viability around 15 % compared with the independent action of MB and GO at a concentration of 20 μg/mL, resulting in cell viability of 15 % and 100 %, respectively. The improved performance of GO-MB can be attributed to increased cell uptake. In this study, the role of GO and MB ratios was also examined, and it was proved that an increased GO/MB ratio promoted the dimerization of MB, which generates less singlet oxygen. Conversely, a reduction in the GO/MB ratio implies the existence of free MB, and it was not evident that GO has a beneficial role in PDT. Based on this concept, the optimal ratio of GO/MB was considered to be 1:1 [125]. The complex GO/MB has also been examined as PS in combined PDT/PTT for the treatment of human cervical cancer cells, SiHa cells. The surfactant compound Pluronic F127 (PF127) was employed for GO/MB functionalization to improve cancer selectivity, preserve photophysical properties, and increase the stability of the complex (Fig. 32 (A) ). In vitro PDT (LED irradiation: 660 nm, 0.5 W/cm2, 10 min) in SiHa cells gave prominence to GO-MB/PF127 as the most efficient PS compared with MB and complex GO/MB. The existence of PF127 facilitates GO-MB/PF127 diffusion to cells and, thus, cell uptake and PDT efficiency. Similar results occurred with in vitro PTT with laser irradiation at 808 nm and a light fluence rate of 0.5 W/cm2 for 10 min. The PS GO-MB/PF127 exhibited the lowest cell viability of 23.6 % compared with MB and GO/MB in synergistic PDT/PTT (Fig. 32 (B)). These observations were visualized with an acridine orange/ethidium bromide fluorescent double staining test. Green fluorescence is attributed to live cells due to acridine orange uptake, while ethidium bromide stains with orange fluorescence cells with a loss in membrane integrity. In the case of cells incubated with GO-MB/PF127, more dead cells were observed compared with free MB or GO regardless of therapy, while it was confirmed that combined PDT/PTT induced increased cell death [126].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo application of graphene oxide in PDT
ce:section.ce:para: Some studies refer to the use of GO in combination with conventional PSs for in vivo PDT. GO can be covalently connected with Ce6 to treat cholangiocarcinoma, and the resultant complex produced improved amounts of ROS and increased cellular uptake compared with pristine Ce6. Studies (homogenous beam: 664 nm, 2 J/cm2) were conducted in SNU478 tumor-bearing mice, and the biodistribution of the administered PS (free Ce6 or GO/Ce6) proved that free Ce6 is accumulated in the liver instead of tumor tissue. However, complexes with GO presented increased fluorescence at the tumor site as well as in the liver and kidney. This contributes to more efficient in vivo PDT with the use of GO/Ce6. It is noteworthy that GO/Ce6 doubled the tumor suppression in comparison with Ce6 alone [127]. In another research, silicon phthalocyanine (SiPc) was covalently conjugated with GO and the targeting agent RGD (arginine-glycine-asparagine) for in vivo combined PDT/PTT. Tumor-bearing mice of the 4T1 type were implemented for in vivo studies with 24 h incubation of the proposed PS, irradiation with a laser at 671 nm, and a light fluence rate of 3 W/cm2 for 10 min. The RGD-GO-SiPc presented enhanced tumor selectivity and photothermal action, while tumor elimination was recorded to a great extent 10 days after the therapy [128].

In the research of Lu et al., the use of a PS based on the linkage of GO with indocyanine green (ICG) for osteosarcoma treatment was reported. GO was functionalized with PEG and folic acid, as well as the ginseng component Ginsenoside Rg3, which exhibits superb anticancer action and is effective against tumor metastasis. Also, the addition of Rg3 facilitated apoptosis and autophagy. Free Rg3, PEG–GO–FA/ICG and PEG–GO– FA/ICG–Rg3 were also examined in vivo in MEG3 tumor-bearing mice upon laser irradiation at 808 nm for 120 s. The use of PEG–GO– FA/ICG–Rg3 as PS in combination with irradiation showed the most increased tumor anticancer activity (Fig. 33 ) [129].

Some other studies report the treatment of melanoma based on PSs with GO. Researchers presented the synthesis of GO chemically modified with cucurbit [7]uril (CB [7]), which enables GO to connect with other molecules via π-π stacking interactions or host-guest ones. In this case, GO-CB [7] was functionalized with the anticancer molecule oxaliplatin (OX), Ce6, the prodrug banoxantrone dihydrochloride (AQ4N), and adamantine-tagged with hyaluronic acid (ADA-HA). In vivo studies were carried out in B16 tumor-bearing mice, proving effective tumor suppression when GO-CB [7]-OX-Ce6-AQ4N-ADA-HA was used as PS. Especially, this PS contributed to temperature rise upon irradiation, enhancement of PDT action of Ce6, while oxygen consumption and possible hypoxia were dealt with by the activation of the prodrug AQ4N. The increased temperature led to oxaliplatin release for the initiation of chemotherapeutic action. Also, the PS was safe, as no major damages were observed in mice's organs via H&E staining [130]. Cheng et al. presented the synthesis and use of nanocomposites based on TiO2, gold nanoclusters (Au NCs), and GO in PDT using simulated sunlight (Fig. 34 A). The role of Au NCs is to produce electrons and transfer them to TiO2. The suppression of electron-hole recombination rate is accomplished through the addition of Au NCs and GO. This implies a decrease in the band gap of TiO2, and hence PDT action is improved with the ability to excite PS at longer wavelength values. The PDT mechanism underlying this PS is based on the electron production of Au NCs upon irradiation with simulated sunlight and the subsequent electron transport to TiO2 and then to graphene. This leads to the formation of holes in both Au NCs and TiO2, as well as free electrons in GO. The successful separation of electron-holes fosters ROS production from the free electrons of GO and holes of Au NCs. In vivo PDT performance (simulated sunlight: 0.1 W/cm2, 20 min), biocompatibility, and biodistribution of the synthesized PSs were examined in vivo in B16F1 tumor-bearing mice. The PS TiO2/Au NCs/GO was accumulated mainly in the liver and spleen, while no pathological changes were recorded in major mice's organs, including the heart, lungs, kidney, liver, and spleen. Regarding the PDT action of these nanomaterials, all the tested PSs were capable of tumor suppression, and the most superior one was observed with the use of TiO2/Au NCs/GO (Fig. 34B–D). It must be mentioned that either the addition of Au NCs or GO in TiO2 resulted in a more efficient tumor decrease in comparison with the action of TiO2 alone. The intratumoral injection of the PS showed improved PDT action in tumor suppression compared with the intravenous one. The PSs had excellent biocompatibility, and no pathological changes or abnormal weight changes were observed during the study [131].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of carbon-based dots in PDT

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) and graphene dots (GDs) are the most representative types of dots based on carbon. These carbon-based nanomaterials are zero-dimensional (0D) with superb electronic and optical properties. CDs were invented in 2004 by Xu et al. during the electrophoretic method for the purification of SWCNTs. In 2008, the first synthesis of GDs was accomplished based on graphene etching by Ponomarenko. Since then, various synthetic methods have been developed for the cost- and time-effective production of GDs, even on a large scale [132–137].

These two types of dots differ significantly in their structure (Fig. 35 ). CDs possess a crystalline or amorphous structure with a carbon core and various surface functional groups. On the other hand, GDs own the structure of a graphene lattice with interlayer defects and surface chemical moieties. The role of these moieties is of the utmost importance for CDs and GDs’ properties, such as aqueous solubility and photoluminescence (PL). The property of PL is also affected by the quantum confinement effect, which is evident in quantum dots due to their small size and describes the spatial confinement of excitons (electron-hole pair) in three dimensions [136,138–140].

The use of CDs and GDs in biological applications is encouraged due to their excellent biocompatibility based on in vitro and in vivo studies. In the literature, their implementation in bioimaging, drug delivery, ion sensing, etc. has been referred. It is pointed out that it is possible to replace semiconductor dots (SDs) with carbon-based ones. The biological use of SDs is a controversial issue due to their basis on toxic metals. The PL of carbon-based dots, along with their hydrophilic nature, has stimulated research interest in their use in PDT. Additionally, surface functional groups facilitate the conjugation of CDs/GDs with traditional PSs to reinforce their photodynamic action as a result of improved cellular uptake and energy transport phenomena that enhance ROS production. Fluorescence of carbon-based dots is also vital, as the combination of treatment with diagnosis (theranostics) is allowed. So, there are references regarding the CDs/GDs synthesis with various precursors’ compounds for their independent use as PSs or for the formation of nanoplatforms with increased selectivity and singlet oxygen generation. During the development of PSs, it must be taken into account the type of cancer. For instance, a lot of studies are focused on the CDs/GDs connection with overexpressing molecules in cancer cells, including folic and hyaluronic acid [13,137,138,144].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro application of carbon-based dots in PDT
ce:section.ce:para: Both CDs and GDs have been applied in vitro as independent PSs to treat various types of cancer. To be more specific, Ahirwar et al. investigated the use of GDs and graphene oxide dots (GODs) for in vitro PDT in B16F10 and MCF-7 cells upon UV irradiation at 365 nm with a light fluence rate of 1.03 mW/cm2. The tested PSs exhibited no dark toxicity in cancer cells, whereas incubation with GDs and UV irradiation for 5 min resulted in cell viability of 9 % and 7 % for MCF-7 and B16F10 cells, respectively. Different response in cell types was recorded in the case of GODs, as in vitro PDT resulted in even lower cell viability in B16F10 (<0.1 %) compared to that of MCF-7 cells (about 7 %). The improved PDT performance of GODs could be attributed to the existence of surface functional groups that facilitate ROS production. Additionally, this research employs graphene-based dots’ fluorescence to study time-dependence in cellular uptake, as well as the localization of the proposed PSs to cytoplasm (Fig. 36 a) [145]. In another case, CDs were chemically functionalized with the aptamer AS1411 to treat HepG2 and HeLa cells with great specificity (Fig. 37 a). The proposed PS was much more cytotoxic upon LED irradiation (400–500 nm, 15 mW/cm2, 10 min) compared with the CDs alone, as it was confirmed with calcein-AM/propidium iodide (PI)-stained cells. In the case of cells incubated with CDs, green fluorescence was observed, indicating live cells, while red fluorescence was evident in cells incubated with aptamer-modified CDs (Fig. 37b). These results were further confirmed with the viabilities of 43 % (81 %) and 41 % (75 %) of HepG2 and HeLa cells, respectively, upon incubation with 200 μg/mL AS1411-CDs (free CDs) (Fig. 37c and (d)) [146].

Doping of carbon-based dots constitutes an attractive approach to their application in PDT without being conjugated with traditional PSs. For instance, CDs can be doped with copper (Cu-CDs) to form a PS with high singlet oxygen generation ability, improved biocompatibility, and fluorescence properties in order to be applied as a diagnostic tool. The in vitro photodynamic action of Cu-CDs was examined in HeLa cells upon LED light irradiation (400–700 nm, 40 mW/cm2), and its concentration- and time-dependent action was proved. This study also underlined the importance of doping in improving fluorescence imaging [147]. Another research studies presented the CDs' doping with sulfur to deal with glioblastoma U87-MG cells and oral squamous cell carcinoma UM1 cells. The U87-MG cells’ incubation with S-CDs showed cell apoptosis, and S-CDs acted as an inhibitor of the PI3K/Akt signaling pathway, which promotes cell proliferation. Furthermore, S-CDs encouraged the activation of the p38/JNK signaling pathway and hence the suppression of BCL-2 and overexpression of BAX, which led to cell apoptosis. S-CDs were more efficient for in vitro PDT (LED irradiation: 420 nm, 25 J/cm, 5 min) compared with PS Ce6 (LED irradiation: 660 nm, 25 J/cm, 5 min), as lower viability of U87-MG cells was recorded at the same concentration of PS. Fluorescent nature of S-CDs allows the study of their localization in cells, proving their accumulation in the cytoplasm. Similar research for in vitro PDT (LED irradiation: 420 nm, 2.8125 W) was carried out with S-CDs incubation in UM1 cells, and the superior performance of S-CDs was proved in terms of cell viability in comparison with that of 5-ALA (Fig. 38 ). Also, researchers mentioned time-dependent cell-uptake of the produced CDs, providing important information regarding their pharmacokinetics. While it was also possible to examine their localization in mitochondria and lysosomes in terms of incubation time [148,149].

Additionally, research studies propose the use of fluorine as a doping agent of carbon-based dots for application in PDT [150–152]. Particularly, Li et al. presented fluorine-doped GDs (F-GDs) for HepG2 cells’ elimination. Researchers produced F-GDs with an oxidative cutting method by using fluorinated graphite as a fluorine source. Doped GDs showed improved aqueous solubility, fluorescence, and ability to generate singlet oxygen compared with the undoped GDs. In vitro cell studies revealed increased biocompatibility of the F-GDs as a result of fluorine doping, while a strong PDT effect was reported for cells incubated with F-GDs and irradiated with LED light (400–700 nm, 40 mW/cm2) [150]. The significance of fluorine as a doping agent is also evident in CDs co-doped with nitrogen [151,152]. Specifically, F,N-co-doped CDs were synthesized based on the pyrolysis of levofloxacin and starch, resulting in enhanced chemical and photophysical properties in comparison with the undoped CDs. The LED irradiation (400–500 nm, 15 mW/cm2) of HepG2 cells confirmed the superior performance of F,N-CDs as PSs [151]. Similarly, in the study of Jiang et al., levofloxacin was also employed for the synthesis of F,N-co-doped CDs, along with neutral red to increase RNA selectivity of the produced nanomaterials. The reported CDs achieved more than 90 % HepG2 cell killing upon LED light irradiation (15 mW/cm2, 8 min) as a result of 1O2-induced precise RNA oxidation damage. Also, this study showed that PDT with this type of F,N-doped CDs as PS was able to cause cell death via pyroptosis [152].

A key factor in PDT success is the intracellular localization of the PS. Based on this concept, researchers have used CDs with improved targeting capability in lysosomes or in the nucleolus. Especially, Zhao et al. proposed CDs capable of accumulating in lysosomes, as it was evident via fluorescence imaging in living cells after CDs' excitation at 457 nm and 800 nm. Lysosomes are cell organelles responsible for maintaining cellular activity and stability. In vitro studies were conducted in HeLa cells for 4 h of PS's incubation and laser irradiation at 635 nm with a fluence rate of 0.1 W/cm2 for 10 min, and the proposed PS was safe in the dark at a concentration range of 12.5–100 μg/mL. This study emphasized the importance of fluence rate and irradiation time in cell viability, while the beneficial role of combinational PDT/PTT was also presented. This role lies in the conversion of received light energy to heat (PTT: laser irradiation at 635 nm, 2 W/cm2) and to ROS upon light exposure at 635 nm (PDT) [153]. The recent study of Cai et al. also presented lysosome-targeted CDs, using metformin and methylene blue as raw materials. In this case, apart from ROS, CDs could generate reactive nitrogen species (RNS) in a light-controlled manner, further enhancing PDT performance against HepG2 cells upon LED light irradiation (400–500 nm, 100 mW/cm2) [154]. One more organelle to monitor is the nucleolus, which constitutes a target in several cancer therapies. Damage to the nucleolus enables non-feasible cell proliferation, and researchers developed nitrogen-doped CDs targeting the nucleolus for in vitro PDT in HeLa cells. Nitrogen doping aims to improve PS's affinity with RNA that is abundant in the nucleolus. The proposed PS exhibited negligible dark toxicity, while cell viability of 22 % was recorded upon laser irradiation at 638 nm with a fluence rate of 1 W/cm2 for 5 min (Fig. 39 a). Similar results were obtained with the live/dead assay with cells stained with calcein-AM/PI. In the case of the dark toxicity study, no fluorescent signal from PI (red fluorescence) was observed, implying the absence of dead cells. Conversely, red fluorescence was evident upon irradiation, and merged images prove the origin of red fluorescence in the intracellular region (Fig. 39b) [155].

Some researchers are focused on the conjugation of CDs/GDs with commonly used PSs, aiming to enhance their photodynamic action and secure their delivery to the target tissue. Ce6 is a common example of PS, which has been examined in vitro in combination with CDs and GDs. Irmania et al. synthesized CDs using waste tea, and manganese (Mn) was implemented as a doping agent to expand the PS's use in MRI. Also, folic acid was linked to the PS in order to improve its affinity for types of cancer cells that overexpress the folate receptors. The PS was biocompatible with the tested cell lines: HeLa cells, HepG2 cells, and B16 cells, as it is shown in Fig. 40 a. Mn-CDs@FA/Ce6 was photodynamically efficient in all cell lines upon laser irradiation at 671 nm with a fluence rate of 1 W/cm2 for 5 min. Particularly, cell viabilities of 10 %, 70 %, and 50 % were recorded for HeLa, HepG2, and B16 cells, respectively, at a Mn-CDs@FA/Ce6 concentration of 500 ppm (Fig. 40b). The superiority of cell death in HeLa cells can be attributed to improved cellular PS's uptake as a result of HeLa cells affinity for folic acid [156]. Similarly, GDs have been investigated as potent PSs in combination with Ce6 for lung cancer treatment (A549 cell line). In this case, HA was implemented as a targeting agent due to the overexpression of HA-sensitive CD44 receptors in cancer cells. In vitro PDT with GDs@HA resulted in cell death of 82 % upon laser irradiation at 670 nm with a fluence rate of 5 mW/cm2 for 5 min. Treated cells with the same concentration of free Ce6 led to 50 % cell death, highlighting the beneficial role of GDs and HA in the photodynamic properties of Ce6 [157].

The importance of adding GDs to the PS phthalocyanine was studied in vitro in MCF-7 cells. Researchers synthesized PSs based on the non-covalent interaction of GDs with neutral and cationic morpholine-substituted phthalocyanines. In vitro PDT (laser irradiation: 680 nm) resulted in higher cell viability in the case of conjugated phthalocyanines compared with that of free phthalocyanines. However, the further modification of complexes with biotin improved the photodynamic activity due to increased biotin receptors in cancer cells, and thus cellular uptake was enhanced. Additionally, complexes with cationic phthalocyanines showed optimal PDT results, as they interact with negatively charged cellular membranes and hence the cellular uptake of the PS was increased [158]. Cationic morpholino-substituted phthalocyanines were also studied in interaction with nitrogen and nitrogen/sulfur GDs (N-GDs, N,S-GDs) for combinational PDT and sonodynamic therapy (SDT). The last type of therapy utilizes ultrasound instead of light to activate drug (sonosensitizers) and produce ROS. In the case of photo-sonodynamic therapy (PSDT), both ultrasound and light initiate the action of the drug. Photophysical studies proved that N-GDs generate more singlet oxygen compared with N,S-GDs as a result of the lower fluorescence quantum yield of N-GDs, which allows more available energy for intersystem crossing. Also, the addition of GDs to phthalocyanine increased the singlet oxygen generation ability and decreased the radicals ∙OH production for the conjugates compared with free phthalocyanine. In vitro PDT, SDT, and PSDT were conducted in MCF-7 cells with laser irradiation at 680 nm (170 J/cm2, 10 min) for PDT and PSDT and ultrasound parameters of 1 MHz, and 1W/cm2 for SDT and PSDT. Both conjugates and phthalocyanine alone exhibited successful cancer cell death, but free phthalocyanine showed better performance in each type of therapy (PDT, SDT, and PSDT). This observation implies that increased singlet oxygen production does not secure improved photodynamic activity, and other factors such as ∙OH production or neutralization of cationic phthalocyanines with GDs addition may also possess a vital role. Furthermore, N-GDs and N,S-GDs were not capable as sensitizers compared with conjugates and free phthalocyanine, despite their ability to produce ROS using light or ultrasound as stimuli. The PSDT modality showed more efficient cell death in comparison with the independent PDT or SDT for all the tested molecules. Fig. 41 presents the cellular morphology under various conditions with the aid of a microscope. Untreated cells with PSs or exposure only to light or ultrasound had an eye-like structure and were surface-adherent. Conversely, application of PSDT to cells treated with 40 μg/mL of free phthalocyanine or conjugates showed cells with an absence of surface adhesion, a reduction in cell size, and circular-shaped cells. Cells treated with N-GDs or N,S-GDs were still surface-adherent [159]. Similar results were also executed upon the conjugation of N-GDs and N,S-GDs with cationic thiazole phthalocyanines to treat HeLa cells and MCF-7 cells with PDT, SDT and PSDT [160].

Some porphyrin derivatives have also been examined in combination with CDs/GDs for in vitro PDT. For instance, Managa et al. studied the addition of GDs to TMPP (meso-tetra(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin tetramethylester), ZnTMPP, and GaClTMPP to boost physicochemical stability and cellular uptake. GDs possess electron donor groups, which facilitate intersystem crossing and thus singlet oxygen generation by the conjugates of GDs with porphyrin derivatives. In vitro studies were carried out in MCF-7 cells with light irradiation with a LED source at 420 nm with a fluence of 40 J/cm2 for 300 s. GDs improved PDT activity, as lower cell viability was recorded in conjugates, and the lowest one (15 %) was observed in the case of GaClTMPP-GDs [161]. In another study, CDs interacted electrostatically with tetraplatinated porphyrin (PtPor) to transport energy from CDs to PtPor via the FRET mechanism (Fig. 42 ). In combination with the EPR effect, the proposed PS secures increased selectivity, and HeLa cells were employed for in vitro PDT research upon irradiation with a 50W xenon lamp, and a long-pass filter at 500 nm with a fluence rate of 6 mW/cm2 for 10 min. The improved action of the complex was proved compared with the free PtPor, showing cell viability around 10 % and 20 %, respectively [162]. Murali et al. proposed the synthesis of CDs using hematoporphyrin (HP) as a precursor compound, aiming to retain the photophysical properties of HP and increase water solubility. The HP-CDs were tested in vitro in MCF-7 cells upon irradiation with a laser at 650 nm and a fluence rate of 50 mW/cm2 for 1 min, resulting in 28 % cell viability, while the correspondent of HP was 36 %. It must also be mentioned that HP-CDs exhibited lower dark toxicity than HP [163].

In the study of Reagen et al., GDs were linked with hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles (hMSNs) to achieve fluorescence imaging along with PDT treatment of murine leukemia cells (RAW 264.7 cells). The role of hMSNs is to inhibit the prolonged accumulation of the PS in the body and thus facilitate the use of hMSNs/GDs in drug delivery applications. In vitro PDT was conducted in RAW 264.7 cells incubated with various concentrations of hMSNs/GDs upon laser irradiation at 785 nm with a fluence rate of 11.81 mW/cm2 for 30 min. The PS did not exhibit drug toxicity for concentrations up to 100 μg/mL, while a linear decrease in cell viability was observed upon PDT with a hMSNs/GDs concentration up to 20 μg/mL. The drug delivery ability of the proposed PS was confirmed with the use of mock drug fluorescein (FITC), and increased fluorescence was shown even in non-toxic concentrations of the PS (Fig. 43 ) [164]. In another case, the hydrophobic PS BODIPY was non-covalently linked with CDs, aiming to improve aqueous solubility, and the PDT performance of CDs-BODIPY (CBNPs) was tested in HeLa cells in comparison with the independent action of CDs and BODIPY (BNPs). Cells were incubated with the PSs for 24 h and irradiated with a laser at 625 nm with a light fluence rate of 16 mW/cm2 for 30 min, showing that the CBNPs had the highest cell death. These results were further confirmed with calcein AM/PI staining of cells, which revealed the absence of dark toxicity and the effective PDT action of BNPs, and mainly of CBNPs, at a concentration of 1 μg/mL [165].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo application of carbon-based dots in PDT
ce:section.ce:para: Some studies present the independent use of carbon-based dots in PDT in vivo. Zhao et al. proposed the synthesis of nitrogen-phosphorus doped CDs (R-CDs) and nitrogen doped CDs (Y-CDs) as bioimaging agents and PSs for lung cancer treatment. Successful tumor suppression in A549 type tumor-bearing mice was proved upon light irradiation at 532 nm, 100 mW/cm2 for 4 min, while the histological examination and study of mice's weight further confirmed the safety of the proposed PS [166]. Based on the anticancer properties of tea polyphenols, researchers used tea polyphenols as raw materials for CDs' synthesis and investigated their potency as PSs against breast cancer. Specially, 4T1 cells were subcutaneously injected into mice in order to perform in vivo studies with the use of tea polyphenols-derived CDs, and then mice were irradiated with LED light (400–500 nm, 15 mW/cm2) for 12 min. The proposed PS accomplished significant tumor elimination without inhibiting the growth of mice. The histopathological analysis further confirmed the absence of pathological changes in organs such as the liver, spleen, lungs, etc. Also, this study highlights the beneficial role of CDs in in vivo fluorescence imaging. Mice injected with CDs showed increased fluorescence signal at 600 nm localized in tumor area, compared with mice injected with PBS solution (Fig. 44 ) [167].

The combinational action of carbon-based dots with traditional PSs has been investigated in in vivo PDT. For instance, titanium dioxide nanotubes (TiO2 NTs) can exhibit improved PDT action upon conjugation with CDs. This is based on the fact that the addition of CDs inhibits the rapid recombination of excitons, facilitates electron transfer from TiO2 NTs, and hence reduces the energy band gap. Also, CDs can execute a water-splitting mechanism, in which they catalyze the water in hydrogen peroxide and then in oxygen. This mechanism is vital for dealing with tumor hypoxia. In vivo studies were conducted in mice bearing cervical cancer U14 with irradiation at 650 nm, a fluence rate of 0.5 mW/cm2 for 10 min, and significant tumor suppression was proved in comparison with TiO2 NTs alone (Fig. 47 ). Additionally, the biocompatibility of CDs/TiO2 NTs was confirmed via hemolysis assay, whereas the mice's weight was studied, and no major damage was observed in the heart, liver, spleen, lung, or kidney via H&E staining [169]. The study of Zheng et al. was also based on the concept of water-splitting mechanism for tumor hypoxia. In this case, CDs were linked with carbon nitride, C3N4, and a polymer consisting of PS PpIX, RGD targeting agent, and PEG. The role of C3N4 is to improve CDs' absorbance in the red region of the electromagnetic spectrum. The nanoplatform was examined in in vivo PDT (He–Ne laser: 630 nm, 80 mW/cm2, 10 min) in 4T1 tumor-bearing mice, showing successful tumor destruction for the proposed PS in comparison with PpIX and conjugate CDs-C3N4. The PS CDs-C3N4 polymer can be preferentially accumulated in tumor within 4 h and can remain to tissue over 36 h after injection, as fluorescence images revealed. Also, no damage was recorded in healthy tissues with H&E staining [170].

In another case, GDs functionalized with PEG were used to deliver Ce6 in cancer tissue. Especially, GDs were linked via disulfide bond with PEG. This type of bond can split and thus release Ce6 in cases of high glutathione levels (GSH), which is a feature of cancer cells. The improvement in PDT performance can be attributed to enhanced cellular uptake of Ce6 due to the use of carrier GDs/PEG. Similarly, in vivo studies were conducted in HeLa tumor-bearing mice irradiated with a laser at 650 nm with a fluence rate of 200 mW/cm2 for 1 h. The PS accomplished tumor reduction from 267 ± 13 mm3 to 118 ± 6 mm3 [171]. Ce6 has also been used in combination with CDs for PDT in 4T1 type of cancer (Fig. 48 ). In this study, CDs were linked with copper ions, which possess a dual role in oxygen production via reaction with hydrogen peroxide and in glutathione consumption, inhibiting the decrease in ROS levels. Mice injected with 4T1 cells were used to study the proposed PS in synergistic PDT/PTT (laser: 660 nm, 0.01 W/cm2, 3 min). The most efficient tumor suppression was recorded in the case of PDT/PTT/chemotherapy with a tumor decrease of 37 %, whereas PDT/PTT resulted in a tumor reduction of 89 % in the control sample. Furthermore, the PS was selective for tumors and eliminated effectively even after 24 h via the kidney and liver, as proved by the increased fluorescence signal in these organs [172].

In the literature, some studies propose the use of CDs/GDs in vivo for synergistic PDT with PTT or chemotherapy. Zhang et al. synthesized nitrogen doped GDs functionalized with hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles, hMSN coated with carbon nitride, C3N4 and further linked with a polymer P-PEG-RGD consisted of hematoporphyrin derivative, PEG and targeting agent RGD. In vivo studies were conducted in 4T1 tumor-bearing mice for 21 days, and combinational PDT/PTT was superior to the independent treatments with the use of PS N-GDs/hMSN. The nanoplatform N-GDs/hMSN/P-PEG-RGD achieved 58.6 % optimal tumor suppression. In vivo fluorescence studies revealed the preferentially accumulation of the proposed nanoplatform to the tumor site with detectable signal just 1 h after injection. However, decrease in fluorescence was observed 24 h post injection, indicating that the nanoplatform undergoes clearance [173].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Current challenges and future directions in the use of carbon-based nanomaterials in PDT
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanomaterials as inorganic PSs possess some advantages over the traditional organic PSs, such as increased light conversion efficiency, improved optical properties, facile surface functionalization, and tunable size. However, inorganic PSs show low biocompatibility, restricting their wide clinical application, and researchers employ modification techniques by conjugating polymers or biomolecules to resolve this issue. These techniques have also been used for the functionalization of carbon-based nanomaterials, such as graphene oxide, fullerenes, or CNTs. Contrary to other carbon-based nanomaterials, carbon dots exhibit increased water solubility, facilitating their biodegradation. It is also important to highlight the fact that inorganic PSs can be used in PDT to develop nanoplatforms, allowing combinational action with biomolecules or other PSs. Some organic PSs, including porphyrin derivatives, exhibit high protein affinity, and this favors their distribution to the whole body, not only to the target tissues. So, these PSs attach to albumin and extend their life span, resulting in prolonged photosensitivity. Incorporation of organic PSs into drug delivery systems could secure targeted distribution of the PS. Therefore, inorganic PSs and especially carbon-based ones show great prospects in future PDT research, taking into account biocompatibility, light conversion, drug delivery systems, and cost-efficient synthesis routes [177–179].

To be more specific, fullerenes are biocompatible and photostable agents, capable of being used in PDT per se or in combination with conventional PSs. The independent use of fullerenes as PSs requires chemical functionalization, which is facile due to their spherical structure [67]. Some studies employ for fullerenes' functionalization diserinol and pyrrolidinium groups with various substitution degrees [74], decationic and deca-tertiary amine chains [75], sugars [78,89], –NH2 groups [80], etc. The main cause of functionalization is to reinforce their aqueous solubility. Except for modification, cage size also constitutes an important parameter [76]. A trend in fullerenes application in PDT is the use of light-harvesting molecules to deal with low fullerenes' absorbance in the red region of the electromagnetic spectrum [68,79]. The conjugation of known PSs with fullerenes is a common strategy that aims to boost intersystem crossing of PSs and thus PDT efficiency. In the literature, BODIPY [78], 5-ALA [88], and BP nanosheets [90] have been conjugated with fullerenes, proving improved photodynamic action in vitro and in vivo compared with the PSs alone. Additionally, fullerenes can be successful agents in nanoplatforms’ development for combined PDT, radiofrequency thermal therapy, MRI, and optical imaging [87].

CNTs exhibit intriguing physicochemical properties and possess high surface area, which is ideal for drug delivery applications. An important asset of CNTs is their ability to absorb NIR, which enables their feasible usage in PTT and for diagnostic purposes [65,95]. Their implementation in PDT as PSs is restricted mainly to conjugation with conventional PSs, such as Ce6 [97,98,107], BODIPY [100], phthalocyanine derivatives [99], mTHPC [104], etc. The addition of CNTs to common PSs showed better PDT performance in comparison with free PSs in most cases as a result of improved cellular uptake. Except for carriers, CNTs can act as independent PSs only upon functionalization with agents, including PEI [106], PVPk30 [106], evans blue [107], chitosan [103], etc. It is noteworthy that there are limited in vivo PDT studies of CNTs due to their possible toxicity. This implies the need for further future research regarding CNTs' toxicity and possible crucial factors. For instance, length and diameter are considered major parameters for CNTs’ toxicity. Also, the surface and purification of CNTs from impurities constitute key factors for PDT efficiency [95]. The beneficial role of CNTs is evident in synergistic PDT with PTT or chemotherapy [104,105,107].

GO and rGO have been applied in PDT as carriers for other PSs or chemotherapeutic drugs. They possess increased aqueous solubility and are considered non-toxic agents. Particularly, their high surface area as well as their π-π graphene structure allow easy conjugation with aromatic structures such as PSs and the formation of nanoplatforms [63,113,114]. Research studies showed the addition of GO/rGO to PPa [117], TMPyP [121], BODIPY [122,123], methylene blue [124–126], Ce6 [127], etc., aiming to increase hydrophilicity and absorbance in the visible region. The biological action of GO is affected by surface, zeta potential, and size [114]. This type of carbon nanostructure has also been successfully implemented in PDT in combination with PTT or chemotherapy [119,120,126,130].

Carbon and graphene dots are a new addition to the carbon-based nanomaterials family, and they have already shown their potential as photodynamic agents. Great optical and electronic properties, water solubility, and photoluminescence foster their usage in biological applications and theranostics. However, they absorb in the ultraviolet region, which limits the available depth of tissue penetration. Researchers deal with this issue through carbon dots’ conjugation with agents such as carbon nitride or with the selection of suitable synthesis methods, precursor compounds, and functionalization techniques [13,17,137,170]. Especially, carbon-based dots can be easily functionalized with doping agents such as nitrogen [159,160,166], sulfur [148,149,159,160], manganese [156], phosphorus [166], and fluorine [150–152] to improve their photophysical properties and hence their usage in fluorescence imaging and in PDT. A lot of studies present their successful independent use as PSs in PDT both in vitro and in vivo, while the abundant surface functional groups allow their conjugation with traditional PSs. Phthalocyanine and porphyrin derivatives [158–162], Ce6 [156,157,171,172], hMSNs [164,173], BODIPY [165], and TiO2 [169] are some of the most common PSs used in combination with CDs or GDs in PDT. The combinational action resulted in improved PDT efficiency, cellular uptake, and cancer cell selectivity. A popular strategy to use CDs/GDs in cancer treatment involves the synergistic action of PDT with PTT, chemotherapy, and SDT [153,159,160,172–174].

All of the studied carbon-based nanomaterials show great prospect as photodynamic agents, either alone or in conjugation with conventional PSs, without inhibiting their action. In particular, some studies presented the independent use of carbon-based nanomaterials, mainly carbon dots and fullerenes, emphasizing on the selection of synthesis route, precursor compounds and surface functionalization. However, recent studies encourage the use of composite materials in PDT, taking the advantages of existing PSs or targeting compounds. In this case, carbon-based nanomaterials participate in the development of multifunctional nanoplatforms with improved targeting abilities, water solubility, and PDT efficiency. Some of the carbon-based nanomaterials showed tempting physical properties, including fluorescence or NIR absorbance, paving the way for their use in cancer diagnosis apart from treatment. Based on this theranostics concept, carbon-based nanomaterials have been linked successfully with MRI agents like Mn3O4 [115], gadolinium ions [86], or iron oxide nanoparticles [87], and this has contributed to tumor visualization along with cure. Carbon nanostructures exhibit reinforced tumor selectivity due to the EPR effect; however, some studies emphasize the need for further affinity to cancerous tissues and link carbon-based nanomaterials with targeting agents such as folic acid [87,105,118,129,156,168], HA [98,130,157,174,175], integrin ανβ3 [118,176], spermine [100], RGD [128,171,173,176], aptamer AS1411 [146], etc. The suitable option for the targeting agent lies in the special features of cancer cells, like the overexpression of some molecules’ receptors. Additionally, the large surface area promotes the use of carbon-based nanomaterials as carriers of hydrophobic drugs and their gradual release to target tissues. The implementation of carbon-based nanomaterials in combinational PDT with other cancer treatments (PTT, chemotherapy, SDT, catalytic therapy, and radiofrequency therapy) proved their potential in a general cancer therapeutic scheme.

Another important aspect of the use of carbon-based nanomaterials in PDT and other biologically related applications is the need for biodegradability. Nanomaterials can interact with biological components of living organisms, such as proteins, and thus interfere in physiological processes. So, many research groups focus on the study of nanomaterials’ toxicity and accumulation in organs. In particular, the biocompatibility of carbon-based nanomaterials is tested with in vitro and in vivo studies, mainly in terms of cell viability or cytotoxicity, toxicity in organs, and immune response. For a long time, researchers considered that carbon-based nanomaterials, especially CNTs and GO, are non-biodegradable due to their inert graphitic sp2 structure. However, recent research studies confirm the successful biodegradation of these materials with enzymatic or oxidative processes, which facilitates their fragmentation and clearance from the body [180,181].

The most common mechanisms for CNTs’ toxicity include oxidative stress, disruption of cellular membranes, disorder of metabolism, and triggering of transcription factors. Crucial parameters for possible toxicity are size, surface area, and purification. To be more specific, small size and large surface area are associated with increased toxicity in CNTs. Furthermore, SWCNTs are more toxic compared with MWCNTs. Route of administration also affects toxicity, with subcutaneous delivery of CNTs to be considered a safer route than intraperitoneal or intravenous administration. CNTs are generally not prone to degradation and can accumulate in tissues for long periods [180,181].

Similar to CNTs, GO is generally considered a non-biodegradable material, and some studies focus on evaluating its possible toxicity to cell and animal models. Chemical functionalization, size, shape, and synthetic pathways are some determining factors for the toxicity of GO and rGO. Smaller GO with uniform size exhibit superb biocompatibility with high cellular uptake, while surface functional groups have a major role in influencing toxicity [180,181].

Enzymatic degradation of fullerenes is also feasible with the formation of hypochlorous acid, transforming fullerenes into products with several functional groups such as hydroxyl, epoxy, or carbon-chlorine. The C60-cage remains intact even after the photo-chlorination [182,183]. Taking into account the fact that carbon dots constitute a recent addition in the carbon nanostructures family, there are limited studies regarding their metabolism over time. Also in this case, nanomaterials’ biodegradation is catalyzed by enzymes, such as lipase. However, their rich surface chemistry, along with their hydrophilic nature, facilitates their rapid clearance and inhibits accumulation in organs like the liver or spleen [184,185].

Recent research interest around carbon-based nanomaterials' biodegradation focuses on the development of new peroxidase enzymes and nanomaterials' surface engineering. Specially, the most common approaches include the synthesis of nanostructures that facilitate degradation via easily cleavable disulfide cross-linker or with the addition of enzyme ligands. The latest increased the catalytic performance of the enzyme. The hydrophobic nature of CNTs and GO limits their metabolism over time, resulting in aggregation and inhibition of enzyme's action due to poor accessibility. Functionalization of nanomaterials also has an important role in enzymatic degradation. For instance, functionalization of carbon-based nanomaterials with negatively-charged carboxylic acid groups facilitates the approach to the enzyme's active site. Also, modification with carboxylic acid groups and other hydrophilic functional groups enhances dispersibility, reduces aggregation, and thus improves interaction with living organisms. An increase in oxygen content also contributes to rapid and effective enzyme degradation. Another aspect includes the conjugation of CNTs to biomolecules like proteins or antibodies with the aim to secure selective targeting and reduce the required amount of PS. General, functionalization affects tissue absorption of carbon-based nanomaterials, distribution, and clearance from the body [180,181,186].

A potential clinical application of carbon-based dots as PSs in PDT requires scrutiny of their metabolism pattern to prevent accumulation in major organs and potent chronic health issues. Many of the in vivo studies presented in this review article studied the metabolism of carbon-based dots, and differences in terms of CDs' tumor accumulation and retention time in mice were reported [168,170,172,173,175,176]. Carbon-based dots' cores are reluctant to modification within the bodily systems and therefore are considered to be excreted of the body without being metabolized. So, surface chemistry is a determining factor of CDs’ metabolism and highlights the fact that it is sufficient to remove only the functional groups of CDs with high affinity to macromolecules, not the whole particle [187].

Another fundamental aspect of carbon-based dots' metabolism is their size, because it determines the following metabolism pathway. In general, the small size of CDs allows for their rapid excretion from the blood circulation, mainly with the aid of the kidney or liver. Carbon-based dots with larger size or with high affinity of surface functional groups to the heart, lungs, and brain may result in longer retention times in these areas. Carbon-based dots with size up to 8 nm can filter through the kidney filtration system, including fenestrated endothelium, glomerular basement membrane, and epithelial podocytes, and thus their renal clearance is facilitated. On the other side, CDs with larger size remain still in the blood and require further processing in the liver. Similar results are also available about the role of CDs’ size in their liver metabolism [187]. For instance, the study of Nurrunabi et al. studied GDs with diameters of 5, 7, 12, and 21 nm and proved the slower excretion of GDs with increasing size [188].

Particle charge is another important factor for the efficient CDs' metabolism through the kidney and liver. Particularly, renal routes accelerate the excretion of positively charged molecules, because the fenestrated endothelium is negatively charged. Therefore, electrostatic repulsion forces delay the renal excretion of also negatively charged proteins such as glycoproteins or negatively charged particles, like CDs. In this case, particles with strong negative charge are metabolized in the liver [187]. Liang et al. compared the excretion of mercaptosuccinic acid-capped SQDs with a negative charge and polyethylenimine-conjugated CDs with a positive charge. In this instance, no biomolecules’ interferences were evident, as both types did not have affinity to proteins, so particle charge was a major parameter. Positively-charged CDs were excreted into the urine, while negatively-charged were located in the liver [189].

Additionally, carbon-based dots with nontoxic hydrophilic groups do not need further modification for body clearance. However, in the case of hydrophobic entities in CDs, hepatic microsomal enzymes are responsible for the oxidation, reduction, and hydrolysis of these entities to enable renal or hepatic excretion. Also, the degradation with the aid of enzymes can lead to a decrease in the carbon core's size and thus facilitate renal clearance of CDs [187]. Martin et al. confirmed the successful enzymatic degradation of GDs using human myeloperoxidase and eosinophil peroxidase in the presence of H2O2 [190]. Metabolism rate is also influenced by administration routes. Carbon-based dots are generally administered through subcutaneous, intravenous, or intratumoral routes. Intravenous injection mode is preferred over other non-injectable routes for laboratory tests, as it secures rapid circulation and prevents CDs' interaction with biomolecules, such as proteins, hormones, vitamins, or plasma, which may lead to alterations in surface chemistry and properties. However, the selection of the appropriate injection type depends on the desired application [187].

Based on the above, there is a need for long-term studies to structure a safety profile for carbon-based nanomaterials with data about chronic effects, distribution, and possible accumulation in organs and metabolism. Deep understanding of carbon-based nanomaterials’ interaction with living organisms, as well as the definition of standardized synthetic and modification protocols, could pave the way for their clinical application as PDT PSs [180,181,186].

This review presented the role of fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide, and carbon-based dots in PDT as PSs. Initially, the fundamentals of PDT, along with the recent trends and challenges, were addressed with emphasis on PS's development. Insufficient PS's distribution at the tumor site, aggregation, tumor hypoxia, and reduced biocompatibility draw research interest in new structures, agents, or therapeutic protocols. Nanotechnology provides a variety of novel materials with enticing properties and time- and cost-effective synthesis procedures. Especially, carbon nanomaterials are versatile materials with a large surface area, biocompatibility, and ease of chemical modification. All these features encourage their use in biological applications, including PDT, while tumor imaging is also possible, improving further treatment selectivity. Based on the presented studies, carbon-based nanomaterials are implemented in PDT alone or in conjugation with other PSs. Fullerenes and carbon-based dots can be used as independent PSs in PDT, demanding proper chemical modification with stabilizing agents or dopants to increase hydrophilicity, photoluminescence properties, and biocompatibility. All of the examined carbon-based nanomaterials allow interaction with traditional PSs, and a lot of studies underline the beneficial role of carbon nanostructures in safe PS's delivery, improved cellular uptake, and PDT efficiency. An important aspect of this review constitutes the use of carbon-based nanomaterials in PDT in combination with other treatment modalities. Apart from fullerenes, all of the studied nanomaterials have been used in combinational PDT/PTT, which is based on tumor destruction due to the production of ROS and heat. Particularly, carbon nanotubes can easily participate in this combined therapy as a result of their absorbance in the NIR region. Furthermore, the use of carbon-based nanomaterials has been examined in chemotherapy synergistically with PDT, in which chemotherapeutic drugs are delivered with the aid of carbon-based nanomaterials and light irradiation is applied. In this case, carbon nanostructures have a dual role as PSs and as drug carriers. All of the above carbon-based nanomaterials have shown their potency in vitro and in vivo PDT studies so far. Therefore, it is postulated that the application area of carbon-based nanomaterials will be broader in the future, and strong preclinical evidence may result in further research in the clinical practice of carbon-based nanomaterials in cancer treatment.

Alexandra Karagianni: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Eleni Alexandratou: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Mauricio Terrones: Writing – review & editing. Konstantinos V. Kordatos: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.
